insights.problem: Templating substitutions create new records; without passing lenient explicitly, validation/record-add strictness could conflict with templating’s intended behavior.
insights.root_cause: Templating processor previously created `record.copy()` then assigned `value(s)` manually, without guaranteeing lenient propagation in the copy/new record flow.
insights.solution: Updated to call `record.copy(values=new_values, lenient=lenient)` or `record.copy(value=new_value, lenient=lenient)` when substitutions change record values.
insights.lesson_learned: When processors mutate records, ensure the leniency flag is preserved through both copy and add_record operations.

insights.title: Templating processor updated to work with alias zones via process_source_and_target_zones
insights.problem: Templating processor was not applying correctly for alias zones, leaving templates in the alias process copy unprocessed.
insights.root_cause: Processor used `process_source_zone`, which runs too early in the pipeline for alias processing; alias copies were created from a desired state that already contained unprocessed template strings (or where templates were not applied in the correct copy).
insights.solution: Switched to `process_source_and_target_zones(self, desired, existing, provider)` so templating runs later during planning changes, and returns `(desired, existing)` so the manager’s plan-based alias flow has the templated values in the correct desired copy.
insights.lesson_learned: For processors that must affect values used during alias expansion/copying, ensure the processor hook runs at a pipeline stage that operates on the exact desired copy used for aliasing.

insights.title: Introduce trailing_dots parameter: templating built-in FQDN variables respect trailing dot stripping
insights.problem: Templating variables like `{zone_name}` and `{record_fqdn}` always included/depended on trailing dot handling, making it impossible to produce correctly formatted output when the system uses trailing-dots-disabled conventions.
insights.root_cause: Built-in template variables were generated with `rstrip('.')`/no toggle, and the templating logic lacked a configurable `trailing_dots` behavior aligned with the templating processor’s configuration.
insights.solution: Added `trailing_dots` parameter to `Templating.__init__`, defaulting to `True`. When disabled, the processor removes the final dot from built-in context variables (`zone_*` and `record_*_fqdn`) and updates template parameter building accordingly.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding a configuration switch, update both zone-level and record-level variable generation consistently, and add tests that validate full parameter dictionaries (not just final values).

insights.title: Adjust trailing_dots behavior when disabled to avoid off-by-one truncation across parameters
insights.problem: The initial `trailing_dots` implementation behaved incorrectly for certain variable values, particularly around how the last dot was removed and when variables were pre-sliced.
insights.root_cause: The processor’s handling of FQDN variables when `trailing_dots` was disabled relied on different slicing points in zone_params vs record_params (and/or relied on earlier `rstrip` behavior), leading to mismatched expectations between zone-level and record-level variables.
insights.solution: Refactored the templating parameter construction so that zone and record FQDN variables are derived from base values and only then truncated when `not self.trailing_dots` (using explicit `[:-1]` when disabling). Also normalized template function and param builder flow.
insights.lesson_learned: For string normalization options (like trailing dot removal), prefer a single source of truth for the base value and apply transformations in one place to avoid inconsistent parameter states.

insights.title: Improve error messages for undefined template parameters
insights.problem: When a template referenced an unknown placeholder (e.g., `{bad}`), errors were not specific to the record and parameter name, making debugging difficult.
insights.root_cause: KeyError propagated from `.template(params)` calls without contextual information about which record caused the failure.
insights.solution: Added `TemplatingError` and wrapped templating evaluation: catch `KeyError` during `value.template(params)` and raise `TemplatingError(record, 'undefined template parameter "{...}" in value')` with record fqdn context.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding templating/formatting features, convert low-level exceptions (KeyError) into domain-specific errors that include record identity and the missing placeholder name.

insights.title: Change semantics of templating built-in FQDN variables by removing trailing dots
insights.problem: Templates using `{zone_name}` and `{record_fqdn}` unexpectedly gained/kept a trailing dot (or vice versa) versus earlier expectations; this broke template output format.
insights.root_cause: Built-in variables previously used values as-is; the change explicitly removes the final dot using `rstrip('.')`, altering output strings from including `.` to excluding it.
insights.solution: Updated `Templating` to set `zone_name`, `zone_decoded_name`, `zone_encoded_name`, and `record_*fqdn` variables with `.rstrip('.')`, and updated tests accordingly.
insights.lesson_learned: String normalization tweaks to template variables are effectively API changes for users; document and provide configuration toggles or migration notes when possible.

octodns/zone.py.short_description: Zone container with record validation, copy-on-write, and diff computation
octodns/zone.py.description:
This module defines the Zone domain object, which holds DNS records for a single DNS zone and enforces zone-level invariants when records are added/removed. It also computes a set of Change objects required to transform an existing Zone into a desired Zone for a particular provider target, taking into account record-level include/exclude filters and provider support. The exceptions in this file (SubzoneRecordException, DuplicateRecordException, InvalidNodeException, InvalidNameError) exist to signal specific invariant violations with additional context from the offending records.

A key design decision is that Zone supports copy-on-write semantics. A Zone can be shallow-copied (via Zone.copy(), defined further down the file) such that it shares state with an origin until the first mutation triggers hydration (via Zone.hydrate()). To make that work, the class keeps an internal `_origin` reference; `records`/`root_ns` read from `_origin` when present. When modifying, the code hydrates to detach state. The `_records` data structure is a `defaultdict(set)` keyed by the IDNA-encoded record name, where each set represents all records at the same node. This enables efficient duplicate detection and CNAME coexistence detection within a node.

Validation behavior centers on three rules:
1) sub-zone boundaries: records belonging in configured `sub_zones` are rejected for the parent zone except for NS/DS at the exact boundary (handled inside Zone.add_record),
2) duplicate detection: adding a record with the same name and type as an existing record raises DuplicateRecordException unless replace=True,
3) CNAME coexistence: if a CNAME is present at a node, it cannot coexist with any other record types at the same node; this raises InvalidNodeException unless lenient behavior applies (either via method parameter lenient or record-level lenience). DNS standards/IDNA handling includes validation of zone name format in __init__ and correct hostname extraction in hostname_from_fqdn using both UTF-8 and IDNA regex matching.

key_constructs.name: SubzoneRecordException
key_constructs.purpose: Raised when a record is added to a zone but should belong to a configured sub-zone
key_constructs.reasoning: Callers rely on this to differentiate sub-zone boundary violations from duplicates/CNAME rules; it captures record.context in the error message when present.

key_constructs.name: DuplicateRecordException
key_constructs.purpose: Raised when attempting to add a duplicate record (same name and type) to a zone
key_constructs.reasoning: It stores both existing and new records and formats context differences in the exception message, which is asserted in tests.

key_constructs.name: InvalidNodeException
key_constructs.purpose: Raised when CNAME coexistence with other record types at the same node is detected
key_constructs.reasoning: It identifies invalid DNS configurations at a specific record/node boundary and includes record context when available.

key_constructs.name: InvalidNameError
key_constructs.purpose: Raised when a zone name is invalid (missing dot, double dots, whitespace)
key_constructs.reasoning: Zone name correctness is foundational; tests and other code assume a normalized IDNA internal representation.

key_constructs.name: Zone
key_constructs.purpose: Container and state-transition object for records in a DNS zone
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the primary abstraction used across sync/planning: providers populate zones, processors transform them, and Zone.changes computes diffs for plan/safety logic.

key_constructs.name: add_record
key_constructs.purpose: Add a record with validation, supporting replacement and lenient modes
key_constructs.reasoning: Zone mutations must respect sub-zone boundaries, duplicates, CNAME rules, and root NS caching; lenient behavior must be applied consistently across branches.

key_constructs.name: owns
key_constructs.purpose: Determine whether the zone owns a given FQDN for a record type, respecting sub-zones
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to decide whether record placement is valid/owned during pipeline stages; NS ownership at the sub-zone boundary is a special case.

key_constructs.name: hostname_from_fqdn
key_constructs.purpose: Extract hostname portion from an FQDN with correct IDNA/UTF-8 handling
key_constructs.reasoning: Sub-zone ownership and name comparisons depend on consistent normalization (IDNA vs UTF-8) across sources.

key_constructs.name: changes
key_constructs.purpose: Compute the list of Create/Update/Delete changes from this (existing) zone to a desired zone
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements the diff logic used by planning and by provider targeting/safety checks; must respect ignored records, included/excluded lists, and target supports().

key_constructs.name: remove_record
key_constructs.purpose: Remove a record from the zone, hydrating if needed
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by Zone.apply(changes) and by tests to manipulate zone state for diff validation; root NS cache behavior is part of correctness.

key_constructs.name: records
key_constructs.purpose: Return the set of all records in this zone, considering copy-on-write origin state
key_constructs.reasoning: Many operations depend on the canonical record set; if hydration/origin is mishandled, diffs and planning will be wrong.

key_behaviors: validates zone name format and normalizes internal IDNA representation
adds records with enforced sub-zone boundary rules and CNAME node invariants
supports replace=True to overwrite duplicates
supports lenient modes to warn instead of raising for certain validation failures
extracts hostname from FQDN with correct IDNA vs UTF-8 matching
computes changes (Create/Update/Delete) between existing and desired zones for a target provider
hydrates copy-on-write zones before mutation

pitfalls.mistake: Changing sub-zone boundary logic without preserving the exact NS/DS-at-boundary exception semantics
pitfalls.consequence: Records will be rejected/accepted incorrectly, breaking subzone placement behavior and plan outputs; tests cover DS vs below-sub cases.
pitfalls.prevention: When editing, ensure the conditional branches for exact sub-zone name vs below-sub are preserved for NS/DS and that the dot-boundary comparisons remain intact.

pitfalls.mistake: Applying lenient behavior inconsistently between Zone.add_record's lenient parameter and record-level record.lenient
pitfalls.consequence: Either strict mode may silently allow invalid configs, or lenient mode may still raise exceptions; this is explicitly covered by commit history and tests.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the raise-vs-warning gating that considers both lenient arguments and record.lenient in each validation branch.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying IDNA/UTF-8 handling for hostname_from_fqdn or the regex boundaries
pitfalls.consequence: Ownership checks and sub-zone enforcement can misclassify names (especially for UTF-8 zone names or xn-- labels), leading to incorrect record ownership and diffing.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the distinction between idna_encode-based matching and UTF-8 regex matching behavior as currently tested.

pitfalls.mistake: Breaking copy-on-write semantics by changing how records/root_ns are read when _origin is set
pitfalls.consequence: Diffs and plans may compare against stale/shared state, causing unexpected mismatches or mutated origin zones.
pitfalls.prevention: Any edits touching hydration triggers or records/root_ns getters must preserve the origin-vs-local behavior.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering duplicate detection or equality semantics assumptions for Record objects
pitfalls.consequence: DuplicateRecordException may not trigger correctly, or replace=True may not remove the intended prior record; tests assert exception messages and selected record identities.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the set/node storage assumptions: duplicates are detected by record equality and node/type semantics as implemented by Record.__eq__/__hash__.

key_sections: Exception classes: formatting/context behavior used by tests
Zone.__init__: zone-name validation and IDNA normalization; regex setup for hostname_from_fqdn
Zone.owns: sub-zone boundary ownership rules, especially NS at exact boundary
Zone.add_record: sub-zone placement validation + duplicate detection + CNAME coexistence + root_ns caching
Zone.remove_record: hydration + root_ns cache clearing
Zone.changes: diffing logic with ignored records, included/excluded filtering, and target support checks
skip_unless_needed: Deprecated _remove_record: only relevant for backwards compatibility; tests cover _remove_record behavior
Minor cached regex details in __init__: only relevant if name normalization behavior is under investigation

relevant_snippets.description: Covers IDNA lowering/normalization, InvalidNameError checks, and hostname_from_fqdn behavior.
Covers add_record duplicate handling and exception message/context formatting.
Covers Zone.changes diffing and application of changes onto a copy.
Covers sub-zone validation rules, including allowed NS/DS at exact boundary and rejection below boundary unless lenient.
Covers ignored records interactions with changes().

related_files.relationship: Direct unit tests for Zone invariants, diff behavior, IDNA/hostname parsing, and sub-zone ownership/validation boundaries
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to Zone.add_record/owns/hostname_from_fqdn/changes must preserve assertions in these tests.

related_files.relationship: Uses Zone indirectly through Plan and safety logic; relies on changes objects produced by Zone.changes
related_files.reason_to_check: If diffs or ordering/filtering behavior in Zone.changes changes, plan output and safety tests may fail.

related_files.relationship: Manager-level integration tests rely on Zone thresholds/config and planning safety tied to Zone attributes
related_files.reason_to_check: Zone constructor arguments include per-zone thresholds; ensure internal state is preserved across copies/hydration.

insights.title: Apply record-level lenience to sub-zone ownership exceptions
insights.problem: Sub-zone-related ownership exceptions were not lenient when lenience was enabled on the record level.
insights.root_cause: Lenient warnings/errors for sub-zone record placement checks only respected the method’s `lenient` parameter, not the record’s own `record.lenient`.
insights.solution: Stored `new_lenient = record.lenient` and updated conditional checks to warn (instead of raise) when either `lenient` or `new_lenient` is true.
insights.lesson_learned: If both global and per-record lenience exist, ensure both are consulted consistently in all zone validation branches.

insights.title: Warn instead of raise when lenient and CNAME coexists with other records
insights.problem: CNAME coexistence produced errors even when lenient mode should only warn.
insights.root_cause: The CNAME coexistence branch used stricter conditions and did not always route to warning behavior in lenient mode.
insights.solution: Refactored `Zone.add_record` logic so CNAME coexistence raises only when not lenient (and neither existing nor new records are lenient). Otherwise it logs a WARNING.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding leniency, be explicit about raise-vs-warn conditions and ensure the warning path preserves the original invariant meaning.

insights.title: Warn instead of raise for managed sub-zone placement when lenient
insights.problem: Sub-zone placement exceptions were always raised even when lenient mode intended to only relax behavior.
insights.root_cause: Sub-zone validation branches didn’t correctly gate exceptions behind lenient checks.
insights.solution: Updated `Zone.add_record` sub-zone validation to compute an error message and either log a warning (lenient) or raise `SubzoneRecordException` (non-lenient).
insights.lesson_learned: Keep raise/warn symmetry in zone invariant checks; tests should cover both lenient and strict cases.

insights.title: Add extensive docstrings clarifying Zone copy-on-write + validation semantics
insights.problem: The Zone class and its related exceptions had limited in-code documentation, making it harder to correctly use copy-on-write hydration semantics and understand validation boundaries (CNAME/subzone/ownership expectations).
insights.root_cause: Documentation gap: significant behavioral rules (hydration triggers, lenient behavior, sub-zone ownership boundaries, CNAME coexistence rules) were implicit in implementation and tests rather than documented.
insights.solution: Commit 4b847b8 adds comprehensive docstrings to Zone and the key exceptions (SubzoneRecordException, DuplicateRecordException, InvalidNodeException, InvalidNameError), including important notes about hydration/copy-on-write and boundary conditions for ownership/validation.
insights.lesson_learned: When a class has non-obvious semantics (copy-on-write, implicit validation, lenient overrides), document the invariants and “important” boundary behaviors in the public API surface so agents and users don’t need to infer them from scattered tests.

insights.title: Introduce _pre_processing_records to support alias-zone record copying
insights.problem: Alias zone copying required access to unmodified records prior to processor execution.
insights.root_cause: Zone previously only tracked `_records`, which were the same objects updated/used across processor stages, leaving no clean way to reference the 'raw' populated set during alias population.
insights.solution: Added `Zone._pre_processing_records` initialized to `None`, documented as a frozen copy of populated records just before processors run.
insights.lesson_learned: When implementing multi-stage pipelines (populate -> preprocess -> alias expansion -> processor passes), add explicit state hooks rather than overloading existing mutable collections.

insights.title: Introduce Zone.apply(changes) for consistent application of diffs
insights.problem: Multiple components needed a consistent way to apply Create/Update/Delete-style changes onto a zone state but lacked a shared primitive.
insights.root_cause: No general Zone.apply existed; zone reconstruction behavior was duplicated or handled ad-hoc in providers.
insights.solution: Add Zone.apply(self, changes) which iterates changes: Delete removes record, otherwise add_record(change.new, replace=True, lenient=True). Add tests for identity with empty changes and correctness after applying changes.
insights.lesson_learned: Centralize state-transition logic (Zone.apply) and reuse it across providers to reduce divergence and subtle output mismatches.

insights.title: Allow DS records at managed sub-zone apex names in add_record lenient checks
insights.problem: Adding DS records for an exact managed sub-zone name was rejected, even though DS is a valid delegation record for the child zone’s apex.
insights.root cause: Zone.sub-zone record validation treated all non-NS records under the sub-zone name as invalid.
insights.solution: Updated the check to allow record types `NS` or `DS` when the record name exactly matches a managed sub-zone.
insights.lesson_learned: Sub-zone constraints should be explicit about which delegation records are acceptable at the boundary (NS/DS), and tests should cover apex exact-match vs below-sub behavior.

insights.title: Zone constructor signature expanded to carry zone-specific thresholds
insights.problem: Existing code that instantiated `Zone(name, sub_zones)` would not provide threshold values, and copy-on-write/hydration logic needed to preserve these new attributes.
insights.root_cause: Zone now needs to store `update_pcent_threshold` and `delete_pcent_threshold` so that planning safety logic can consult zone overrides.
insights.solution: Extended `Zone.__init__` with optional `update_pcent_threshold=None` and `delete_pcent_threshold=None`, stored them on the Zone instance, and updated `Zone.copy()` to pass the new attributes through.
insights.lesson_learned: When extending core domain objects (like Zone) with new config-driven fields, update all construction sites and internal copy/clone paths to avoid losing state during sync/plan computations.

tests/test_octodns_zone.py.short_description: Unit tests covering Zone record validation, sub-zone rules, diffing, and IDNA behaviors
tests/test_octodns_zone.py.description:
This file contains the primary unit-test suite for octodns.zone.Zone. It validates core invariants implemented in Zone.add_record and ownership semantics in Zone.owns, including IDNA normalization and correct hostname extraction from FQDNs. It also tests Zone.copy-on-write behaviors indirectly via Zone.changes and Zone.apply usage.

The tests emphasize boundary conditions that are easy to regress: duplicate record handling must raise DuplicateRecordException with specific message/context formatting; CNAME coexistence and sub-zone rules must behave differently under strict vs lenient modes. Sub-zone tests are particularly detailed: NS/DS are allowed only when the record name exactly matches the managed sub-zone apex, while other record types (and delegation records below the apex) must be rejected unless lenient mode is enabled.

Finally, the file asserts how Zone.changes interacts with provider targeting: records marked ignored should not affect whether changes are produced; included/excluded lists should filter changes based on target.id; and provider.supports(record) should control whether unsupported record types are included as Create/Delete actions. These tests give a contract for what Zone.changes must compute given existing/desired zones and a target provider.

key_constructs.name: TestZone
key_constructs.purpose: Namespace for Zone unit tests
key_constructs.reasoning: Organizes high-signal test cases for validation and diff behavior, which directly mirror Zone invariants.

key_behaviors: asserts Zone normalizes and stores IDNA zone names
asserts Zone.hostname_from_fqdn strips zone portion correctly for both UTF-8 and IDNA inputs
asserts Zone.add_record raises DuplicateRecordException for same name/type and honors replace=True
asserts exception messages include context details when provided
asserts Zone.changes produces correct Create/Delete/Update lists and can be applied to a copy
asserts Zone.owns implements sub-zone boundary ownership rules
asserts Zone.add_record enforces sub-zone boundary rules with lenient overrides
asserts Zone.changes respects ignored records and provider support filtering

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying Zone behavior related to lenient mode without updating tests expectations about warning vs raise behavior
pitfalls.consequence: Tests may fail because they rely on raising exceptions or allowing additions under lenient conditions.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure any behavioral adjustments preserve the strict/lenient contract that the sub-zone and duplicate/context tests encode.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing exception message formatting or context label text
pitfalls.consequence: These tests assert exact string contents (including line splitting), so small message changes will break them.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat exception message formatting as part of the observable contract in these tests.

key_sections: test_lowering / test_utf8 / test_hostname_from_fqdn: IDNA/normalization contract
test_add_record / test_duplicate_context_handling: duplicate exception behavior and message formatting
test_changes: diff semantics and Zone.apply correctness through copy mutation
test_owns / test_sub_zones: sub-zone boundary enforcement including NS/DS edge cases
test_ignored_records / test_unsupporting: filtering based on ignored and provider.supports
skip_unless_needed: test_deprecated__remove_record: only relevant if maintaining backward compatibility for _remove_record

related_files.relationship: Implements the Zone behaviors validated by these tests
related_files.reason_to_check: Any changes to exception messages, validation gating, ownership logic, or diff filtering must preserve these assertions.

related_files.relationship: Provides per-zone threshold config used in higher-level manager/planning safety tests that depend on Zone fields
related_files.reason_to_check: If Zone constructor signature/threshold attributes are modified, integration tests may fail.

insights.title: Add tests for Zone.apply behavior (no-op and actual mutation)
insights.problem: New Zone.apply behavior needed verification to ensure it mutates zone state correctly and doesn't change records on empty changes.
insights.root_cause: Zone.apply was newly introduced without prior tests.
insights.solution: Test that applying [] preserves records equality and that applying a known changes list makes the copy match the expected target zone and diverge from the original.
insights.lesson_learned: When introducing new state mutation APIs, include both no-op and non-trivial mutation tests to prevent regression.

insights.title: Add zone tests for DS record acceptance boundary conditions
insights.problem: Without tests, managed sub-zone rules could regress and either incorrectly reject DS or incorrectly accept DS below the sub-zone apex.
insights.root cause: Boundary logic is easy to get subtly wrong and is dependent on exact name matching and `lenient` flag.
insights.solution: Added tests verifying: (1) DS at the exact sub name is allowed, (2) DS below the sub name is rejected unless `lenient=True`, and (3) names that merely contain strings matching sub names (no trailing dot boundary behavior) are OK.
insights.lesson_learned: For hierarchical naming rules, tests must cover exact match vs below match and also tricky string containment scenarios.

octodns/provider/plan.py.short_description: Defines Plan safety checks and renderers (logger/json/markdown/html) for planned DNS changes.
octodns/provider/plan.py.description:
This module is the central representation of a provider plan: it takes an existing zone state, a desired zone state, and a list of change objects, and produces both (1) safety validations to prevent overly risky apply operations and (2) multiple human/machine-readable renderings of the plan.

The `Plan` class is also where threshold precedence is enforced. It computes counts of create/update/delete operations from the passed-in `changes` list (which is sorted for deterministic output). It then selects safety thresholds by preferring zone-configured thresholds found on `existing` (if not `None`), otherwise using the provider-level defaults passed into the constructor. The `raise_if_unsafe` method enforces two safety mechanisms: percentage thresholds for updates and deletes when there are at least `MIN_EXISTING_RECORDS` records, and a special-case “root NS change” rule that always forces unsafe when any change targets the zone’s root NS record (`c.record._type == 'NS'` and `c.record.name == ''`).

Below `Plan`, the file defines plan output helpers: `PlanLogger`, `PlanJson`, `PlanMarkdown`, and `PlanHtml`. These consume an iterable of `(target, plan)` pairs and render the plan contents. A decorator (`_custom_fh`) supports writing plan output either to a passed-in file handle (`fh` defaulting to `stdout`) or to a file specified via `output_filename`. Outputs embed structured `Plan.data` in JSON, and render per-change details (operation type, record name, record type, TTL/value, and source where present) for markdown/html and text logging. All renderers include `plan.meta` when present, and the summary line reflects whether meta was set (via the `Meta=` flag in `Plan.__repr__`).

key_constructs.name: UnsafePlan
key_constructs.purpose: Base exception type used to signal that a plan is unsafe to apply.
key_constructs.reasoning: Safety checks in `Plan.raise_if_unsafe()` raise subclasses of this type, so callers can distinguish unsafe-plan failures from other errors.

key_constructs.name: RootNsChange
key_constructs.purpose: Signals that a plan modifies the zone root NS record and should require forced apply.
key_constructs.reasoning: This rule is intentionally special-cased in `Plan.raise_if_unsafe()` because root NS changes are considered high risk.

key_constructs.name: TooMuchChange
key_constructs.purpose: Signals that update/delete percentages exceed configured safety thresholds.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a detailed error message including percentage, thresholds, and record counts, used by tests to validate safety behavior.

key_constructs.name: Plan
key_constructs.purpose: Represents an actionable plan of record changes, including safety validations and serializable data.
key_constructs.reasoning: All safety rules and renderer-facing properties (`data`, `change_counts`, meta/threshold precedence) are defined here; altering behavior impacts both apply safety and output formats/tests.

key_constructs.name: _custom_fh
key_constructs.purpose: Decorator that reroutes plan renderer output to a file when `output_filename` is set.
key_constructs.reasoning: Renderers rely on it to preserve the existing `fh` argument behavior while adding optional file output; tests cover both paths.

key_constructs.name: _PlanFhOutput
key_constructs.purpose: Base class for plan outputs that optionally write to `output_filename`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the `output_filename` field used by `_custom_fh` and keeps output classes consistent.

key_constructs.name: PlanLogger
key_constructs.purpose: Renders plan information into log messages using a specified log level.
key_constructs.reasoning: Tests assert on the exact summary substring produced by `Plan.__repr__`, including `Meta=False` when meta is absent.

key_constructs.name: PlanJson
key_constructs.purpose: Emits machine-readable JSON describing planned changes grouped by target id and desired record name.
key_constructs.reasoning: JSON shape is relied upon by tests (top-level keys and number of items); `Plan.data` is the source of record-change serialization.

key_constructs.name: PlanMarkdown
key_constructs.purpose: Renders plan details as a markdown document with tables for each target.
key_constructs.reasoning: Tests validate presence of specific markdown table rows/fields and summary text.

key_constructs.name: PlanHtml
key_constructs.purpose: Renders plan details as an HTML table grouped by zone and target.
key_constructs.reasoning: Tests validate specific HTML substrings; rendering logic must remain stable across changes.

key_constructs.name: _value_stringifier
key_constructs.purpose: Converts record value(s) into a separator-joined string, including optional geo values.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by markdown/html renderers to display TTL/value and supports both non-geo and geo-aware record objects.

handles_entities: Target (via target.id and target.__str__ used for logging)
key_behaviors: sorts the passed changes list for deterministic plan output
computes Create/Delete/Update counts from the change class names
selects update/delete safety thresholds with zone precedence over provided defaults
raises TooMuchChange when update/delete percentage exceeds thresholds and minimum existing records are met
raises RootNsChange if any change targets the zone root NS record (NS at name '')
exposes a deterministic plan payload via Plan.data: {'changes': [...], 'meta': ...}
renders plan outputs to either an injected file handle (fh) or a filename (output_filename)

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the conditions for the root NS detection (type/name checks) without updating the corresponding behavior expectations.
pitfalls.consequence: Plans involving root NS changes may become incorrectly safe, bypassing the forced-apply requirement.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the exact semantic criteria used by `raise_if_unsafe`: `c.record._type == 'NS' and c.record.name == ''` guarded by `self.exists`.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming the safety thresholds are always the provider defaults (ignoring zone overrides).
pitfalls.consequence: Safety tests and real-world behavior would diverge: `existing.update_pcent_threshold/delete_pcent_threshold` are explicitly meant to take precedence when not `None`.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the zone precedence conditional logic in `Plan.__init__` intact.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying `Plan.__repr__` or the summary string format without coordinating renderers/tests.
pitfalls.consequence: Renderers and `PlanLogger` rely on `str(plan)` (which uses `__repr__`) and tests assert exact substrings like `Meta=False`.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat `Plan.__repr__` as part of the output contract exercised by tests for logger/html/markdown.

pitfalls.mistake: Breaking the JSON schema construction in `PlanJson` by changing grouping keys or using a different nesting layout.
pitfalls.consequence: Machine-consumption expectations and `TestPlanJson` top-level key assertions will fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve that JSON is nested as `data[target.id][plan.desired.name] = plan.data`.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering output file handling semantics for plan renderers (fh vs output_filename).
pitfalls.consequence: Tests that write to `TemporaryDirectory` will fail, and callers may end up writing to the wrong stream.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the `_custom_fh` decorator contract: when `self.output_filename` is not None, open it and inject `fh` into the renderer’s kwargs.

key_sections: Plan.__init__: change sorting, change_counts computation, and threshold precedence logic
Plan.raise_if_unsafe: update/delete percentage enforcement and root NS special-case
Plan.data: serialization contract for JSON output and checksum/integrity consumers
_custom_fh and *_Output.run: file-handle routing and renderer output formats
skip_unless_needed: _PlanOutput and _PlanFhOutput boilerplate
HTML/Markdown row formatting details unless adjusting render output expectations

relevant_snippets.description: Defines sample changes/plans and expected summary substrings used by output tests.
Asserts `PlanLogger` level mapping errors for invalid input.
Asserts HTML empty and summary substring rendering for non-empty plans.
Validates JSON output structure by parsing JSON and checking top-level keys.
Validates markdown rendering includes Create/Update/Delete rows and geo value formatting.
Safety threshold tests: provider vs zone precedence, min existing record gating, and RootNsChange.
Asserts exact Plan.data and underlying Change.data key shapes/types.
Asserts output_filename routing for json/markdown/html and verifies default fh writing behavior.

related_files.relationship: Zone provides decoded_name and optional threshold overrides read by Plan.__init__.
related_files.reason_to_check: When editing Plan threshold selection or metadata behavior, verify Zone’s threshold field types/defaults and how `.copy()` preserves them.

insights.title: Rename plan output path parameter to output_filename and implement decorator-based file handle routing
insights.problem: Plan output classes needed to support per-output files while still allowing default behavior to write to a provided file handle.
insights.root_cause: Initial design used `output_path`; later API preference/consistency led to renaming to `output_filename` and restructuring the file-open logic.
insights.solution: Refactored plan outputs to use a decorator (`_custom_fh`) and introduced `_PlanFhOutput` with `output_filename`. Updated `PlanJson`, `PlanMarkdown`, and `PlanHtml` to write either to an opened filename or the passed `fh`.
insights.lesson_learned: When refactoring output/IO behavior, keep tests for both explicit file and injected file-handle paths to prevent regressions.

insights.title: Replace deprecated logging WARN with WARNING mapping
insights.problem: Deprecated logging constant WARN was used for mapping textual log levels to numeric levels.
insights.root_cause: Use of logging.WARN (deprecated) in PlanLogger level mapping.
insights.solution: Switched import to WARNING and updated the mapping for both 'warn' and 'warning' keys to WARNING.
insights.lesson_learned: Deprecations in Python’s logging constants should be handled holistically: update imports and all level mapping logic to avoid runtime warnings and inconsistencies.

insights.title: Plan supports meta payload and renders it in all output formats
insights.problem: Plan output did not expose any structured data for non-record operations, limiting provider extensibility and observability.
insights.root_cause: Plan.data and renderers only handled the list of record changes; no field existed to carry provider meta.
insights.solution: Extend Plan constructor with meta=None, store it on the instance, include it in Plan.data under 'meta', update __str__ summary to show Meta flag, and render meta in PlanLogger/PlanMarkdown/PlanHtml using pprint.pformat.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding new fields to a core object (Plan), ensure every consumer (data serialization, loggers, markdown/html renderers, safety checks/tests) is updated together to avoid silent omissions.

insights.title: Implement PlanJson output by aggregating plan desired data per target
insights.problem: No JSON plan output existed or output formatting was inefficient/incomplete for machine consumption.
insights.solution: Added `PlanJson` that builds a nested structure keyed by `target.id` and `plan.desired.name`, using `json.dumps` for output.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding machine-readable output, define a stable structure (target id -> record name -> change set) and write tests to validate presence/shape rather than string matching.

insights.title: Zone-level change thresholds take precedence over provider thresholds
insights.problem: Plan threshold selection could be unclear/duplicative, and the intended precedence semantics (zone overrides provider) needed to be expressed more directly.
insights.root_cause: The Plan class was selecting threshold values in a way that did not clearly separate the “zone override” logic from “provider/default fallback”.
insights.solution: Refactored threshold assignment so that if `existing.update_pcent_threshold` / `existing.delete_pcent_threshold` is not None, those values override the provider-level thresholds; otherwise the Plan constructor’s passed-in thresholds are used.
insights.lesson_learned: When introducing precedence rules (zone vs provider vs default), encode them explicitly with straightforward conditional branches to avoid subtle override bugs and to make intent readable.

insights.title: Expose Plan.data for deterministic checksum computation
insights.problem: Checksum support required serializing the plan’s actionable changes in a stable, structured way.
insights.root_cause: Plan previously didn’t provide a concise data representation of changes suitable for hashing.
insights.solution: Add Plan.data property returning {'changes': [c.data ...]} to enable Manager.sync checksum computation.
insights.lesson_learned: If higher-level features (like integrity checks) depend on plan content, provide explicit serialization hooks on core objects.

octodns/manager.py.short_description: Core orchestration for loading config, wiring plugins, processing zones, and running sync/dump plans.
octodns/manager.py.description:
This module defines the `Manager` class, which is the central entry point for octoDNS operations. It reads the YAML config, normalizes key sections (zones, manager settings), instantiates configured providers/processors/plan outputs/secret handlers via dynamic imports, and then coordinates the end-to-end sync workflow across zones and sub-zones. It also contains several helper types used to make plugin execution optionally thread-pooled while preserving a “future-like” interface (`MainThreadExecutor` and `MakeThreadFuture`).

A key responsibility of `Manager` is translating config structures into runtime objects while handling expansion and validation boundaries. `_build_kwargs` walks provider/processor/plan_output/secret_handler config dictionaries, expanding `env/VAR_NAME` style references through the configured secret handlers. The module also contains zone normalization for IDNA: `_config_zones` converts zone keys into an `IdnaDict`, while guarding against ambiguous configuration where both utf-8 and IDNA forms are present. Additionally, `configured_sub_zones()` precomputes a mapping from each configured zone to its sub-zones in IDNA form; this is later used for eligibility filtering and zone traversal.

Within the larger planning/apply pipeline (partially truncated in the provided excerpt), `Manager` includes logic for processors, provider targeting, and plan generation. The helper class `_AggregateTarget` combines multiple targets by intersecting SUPPORTS_* flags and requiring that all targets support a record. It is exercised directly by tests, and it constrains how the rest of the manager decides whether specific records can be planned for a given set of targets.

key_constructs.name: _AggregateTarget
key_constructs.purpose: Represents a composite target across multiple provider targets, only supporting features supported by all.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is used to enforce AND-semantics for SUPPORTS flags across targets; affects record planning eligibility and `supports()` decisions.

key_constructs.name: MainThreadExecutor
key_constructs.purpose: Executor stub that runs submitted functions immediately on the main thread but returns a future-like wrapper.
key_constructs.reasoning: Allows the codebase to uniformly structure “async-like” execution without needing actual thread pools when `max_workers=1`.

key_constructs.name: MakeThreadFuture
key_constructs.purpose: Minimal future-like object that exposes `.result()` by directly invoking the stored callable.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by `MainThreadExecutor` to match expectations from call sites that assume futures.

key_constructs.name: Manager
key_constructs.purpose: Main orchestrator: config loading, plugin instantiation, processor/provider wiring, zone preprocessing, and sync/dump control.
key_constructs.reasoning: Nearly all higher-level behavior in octoDNS (sync/compare/dump; including checksum gating and env/IDNA handling) is mediated here.

key_constructs.name: _build_kwargs
key_constructs.purpose: Recursively builds kwargs for plugin constructors, expanding dicts and resolving env-based secret references.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the boundary where config values become runtime-typed values; it must coordinate with `octodns.secret.environ.EnvironSecrets`.

key_constructs.name: configured_sub_zones
key_constructs.purpose: Computes, caches, and returns the set of sub-zones configured under a given zone name (IDNA form).
key_constructs.reasoning: Impacts eligibility filtering and zone iteration logic; depends on correct IDNA normalization done in `_config_zones`.

key_constructs.name: _config_zones
key_constructs.purpose: Normalizes the `zones` section from config into an `IdnaDict` and validates IDNA/utf-8 duplication.
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents ambiguous zone resolution and ensures downstream lookup and eligibility work consistently.

key_constructs.name: _get_named_class
key_constructs.purpose: Imports a named class from a dotted path and returns (class, module_name, version).
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes error handling for misconfigured plugin classes and version reporting.

key_behaviors: loads YAML config and normalizes configured zones for IDNA
instantiates providers/processors/plan outputs/secret handlers from class paths
expands `env/...` values in plugin config using configured secret handlers
chooses a thread pool or main-thread executor based on `max_workers`
computes and caches sub-zone mappings for eligibility/iteration
aggregates multiple targets and enforces joint support requirements via SUPPORTS_* intersections

pitfalls.mistake: Changing `_build_kwargs` value parsing/expansion behavior without considering `EnvironSecrets.fetch` type coercion.
pitfalls.consequence: Config-derived types may become inconsistent with secret/env behavior, breaking checksum computations, processor/provider parameter expectations, or tests asserting numeric types.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat env expansion and type coercion as a boundary: verify behavior against `tests/test_octodns_secret_environ.py` and Manager tests that depend on env expansion.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying IDNA zone normalization logic (`_config_zones`) without preserving the duplication guard between utf-8 and `xn--` forms.
pitfalls.consequence: Zone selection and eligibility filtering may become ambiguous or wrong; tests for IDNA eligible zones assert specific exception messages.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure any change keeps the invariant: a decoded IDNA zone cannot coexist with its utf-8 equivalent in config.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering executor behavior (thread vs main thread) without maintaining the future-like `.result()` contract.
pitfalls.consequence: Call sites expecting async semantics may break or block; tests that run sync/dump under `max_workers=1` rely on correct behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep `MainThreadExecutor.submit()` returning an object compatible with expected future usage.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing `_AggregateTarget` support semantics or attribute forwarding for SUPPORTS_*.
pitfalls.consequence: Record planning eligibility could change (SUPPORTS_* flags become too permissive or too strict), affecting counts returned by Manager sync/dump tests.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve AND-semantics across targets and the dynamic `SUPPORTS_` attribute handling behavior.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing exception message text/wording for invalid plugin class configs or missing resources.
pitfalls.consequence: Unit tests assert on substring matches or exact strings; mismatch will cause failures.
pitfalls.prevention: When editing validation paths (`_get_named_class`, config parsing, eligibility checks), re-run tests that assert on exact exception messages.

key_sections: Manager.__init__: config normalization + plugin instantiation wiring
_config_zones: IDNA normalization and duplication validation
_config_executor: max_workers to thread pool vs MainThreadExecutor
_build_kwargs: recursive env expansion via secret handlers
configured_sub_zones: cached computation of sub-zone sets for eligibility/iteration
_AggregateTarget: target support aggregation semantics
skip_unless_needed: MakeThreadFuture.result: trivial wrapper used by executor

relevant_snippets.description: Manager initialization error tests for missing/bad provider classes and missing provider config/env.
TestManager.test_idna_eligible_zones asserts IDNA normalization impacts eligible_zones errors.
TestManager.test_enable_checksum asserts checksum gating behavior and exact exception message suffixing.
TestManager.test_aggregate_target exercises _AggregateTarget SUPPORTS_* semantics and supports().
Dump behavior tests (unknown source handling, zone='*' expansion, unknown zone handling).

related_files.relationship: Secret handler used by Manager as the default env handler ('env'), including type coercion rules.
related_files.reason_to_check: If env value parsing changes, it affects Manager plugin config kwargs and tests around env expansion.

related_files.relationship: YamlProvider is used by tests and depends on Manager wiring for directories and sources/targets config.
related_files.reason_to_check: To understand how plugin config built by Manager is consumed during dump/sync tests.

related_files.relationship: Primary test suite asserting Manager behavior, counts, and exact exception text.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral change in Manager (validation, IDNA handling, checksum gating, processor wiring) will be detected here.

insights.title: Backward-compatible lenient kwarg support across processor and plan hooks
insights.problem: Adding `lenient` to processor/provider hook signatures could break custom processors or targets that don’t accept the new kwarg.
insights.root_cause: Method signature changes in a plugin ecosystem cause `TypeError: unexpected keyword argument ...` for older classes.
insights.solution: Wrapped calls to processor methods (`process_source_zone`, `process_plan`, `process_source_and_target_zones`) and provider target `.plan(..., processors=..., lenient=...)` in try/except. On lenient-related `TypeError`, emit a deprecation warning and retry without `lenient`. Additionally handled older provider signatures around `processors` vs no `processors` kwarg.
insights.lesson_learned: When evolving public extensibility points, implement robust fallback behavior and ensure the fallback is only triggered for the specific, expected error mode.

insights.title: Use dedicated Checksum logger so checksum is visible in --quiet mode
insights.problem: Computed checksum logging was suppressed/hidden when running in --quiet mode because it used the main logger level behavior.
insights.root_cause: Checksum info was logged via the default manager logger, which is affected by quiet-mode filtering.
insights.solution: Created a dedicated logger named 'Checksum', explicitly set its level to INFO, and logged checksum via that logger so it bypasses quiet-mode suppression rules applied elsewhere.
insights.lesson_learned: When quiet modes exist, log important operational identifiers (like checksums) via dedicated loggers that can be controlled independently from general output noise.

insights.title: Manager adds processor support to octodns-dump via shared _get_processors logic
insights.problem: octodns-dump previously lacked processor support, so filters/transforms couldn’t be applied during dump generation.
insights.root_cause: dump code path didn’t reuse sync’s existing processor wiring; processor resolution logic was embedded in sync flow rather than shared.
insights.solution: Commit 47b9bf2 introduces _get_processors(decoded_zone_name, config), uses it in dump(), and applies `processor.process_source_zone(...)` before target planning. Also adds tests for filtered dumping and unknown processor handling.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding feature parity across commands (sync vs dump), factor shared resolution logic (processor lookup, validation) into shared helpers to avoid divergence.

insights.title: Fix logging issues in Manager: incorrect log prefix and generator-to-list conversion
insights.problem: Logging output was incorrect/misleading (wrong prefix) and one log call passed a generator expression, risking unreadable or delayed evaluation.
insights.root_cause: Log statement string prefix drift and generator usage within `%s` logging context.
insights.solution: Commit 3b6fdc1 changes log prefix for _get_sources and converts `(s.id for s in found_sources)` to `list(...)`.
insights.lesson_learned: Avoid passing generators into logging placeholders; materialize when needed. Keep log prefixes aligned with the method/function to make debugging easier.

insights.title: Alias zones required pre-processor snapshot of records for processors like Templating
insights.problem: When using processors on alias zones, processors would act on already-processed/copy records rather than the raw populated records, causing templating (and potentially other record-transforming processors) to not behave as expected during alias processing.
insights.root_cause: Manager copied alias records from `desired._records` after some processor steps had already modified state, so processors did not see the intended pre-processed snapshot of records; moreover, alias population occurred before the full processor flow later in planning.
insights.solution: Manager now deep-copies the populated zone records into `zone._pre_processing_records` before calling processors (`Manager._populate_and_plan`), and alias population copies from `desired._pre_processing_records` (via changing usage from `desired._records` to `desired._pre_processing_records`). Additionally logs `sync: alias_of=...`.
insights.lesson_learned: If a processor changes record fields based on zone/record context, and alias zones duplicate records, capture a frozen snapshot of the records immediately before processor execution and ensure alias population uses that snapshot—not the post-processor mutated set.

insights.title: Deprecated legacy populate/plan parameter fallbacks
insights.problem: Some call sites may pass legacy params (lenient to Source.populate, processors to Provider.plan) that are no longer supported, potentially leading to unexpected behavior or future removal.
insights.root_cause: Manager contains fallback handling when it detects those keyword arguments aren’t accepted by the underlying classes.
insights.solution: Emit deprecation warnings (deprecated(..., stacklevel=99)) when calling populate without lenient support and when calling plan without processors support, while keeping current fallback behavior.
insights.lesson_learned: Maintain backward compatibility with runtime shims but loudly deprecate them with correct stacklevel so users can locate the call site.

insights.title: Don't crash when config keys are present but set to None
insights.problem: Manager initialization (and downstream config parsing) could raise exceptions when users explicitly set optional config keys to null/None (e.g., `manager: {}`, `processors: null`, `plan_outputs: null`).
insights.root cause: Direct `.get(...)` usages assumed values were proper containers; when the config YAML had keys with value `null`, those expressions returned `None` and later code treated them like lists/dicts.
insights.solution: Changed many config reads to coerce `None` to empty container defaults (e.g., `manager_config = self.config.get('manager') or {}`, `secret_handlers_config = ... or {}`, `global_processors = ... or []`, `plan_outputs_config = ... or {}`, `processors_config = ... or {}`, etc.). Also adjusted executor max_workers handling with `or 1`.
insights.lesson_learned: In config-driven systems, treat `null` as an allowed value for optional keys and normalize it early (at the boundaries) to avoid scattered `None` handling and later type errors.

insights.title: Stop converting env-derived kwargs in Manager; defer to EnvironSecrets
insights.problem: Manager performed ad-hoc numeric conversion for values in `_build_kwargs` (float if dot else int). A regression caused incorrect handling and led to inconsistent parsing outcomes versus secret/env behavior.
insights.root_cause: Type conversion logic was duplicated: sometimes Manager coerced strings in kwargs, sometimes EnvironSecrets did its own coercion. Removing/adjusting one path without aligning the other led to inconsistent types.
insights.solution: Removed the conversion block from Manager and kept conversion in EnvironSecrets (`octodns/secret/environ.py`) using consistent `'.' in v` detection logic.
insights.lesson_learned: If secrets/env values are used as typed configuration, ensure there is exactly one canonical conversion layer; deleting duplicated conversion logic can be the safer fix when regressions appear.

insights.title: Raise ManagerException when a passed zone is missing from config
insights.problem: Passing an explicit zone list to Manager.sync could fail to signal that a requested zone wasn’t present in the loaded config, leading to confusing errors later (or silent no-ops).
insights.root_cause: Manager.sync iterated zones based on the loaded config mapping but did not guard against a None/absent zone entry in that internal structure.
insights.solution: During sync iteration, if config is None for a zone_name, raise ManagerException('Passed zone: ... not found in config').
insights.lesson_learned: Validate requested inputs early (e.g., CLI-selected zones) and produce a targeted error message rather than allowing later stages to fail ambiguously.

insights.title: Support nested env var expansion in provider/processor config
insights.problem: Only top-level provider/processor keys supported env/<VAR> expansion; nested dict structures were not expanded.
insights.root_cause: Manager._build_kwargs/config parsing logic only checked for 'env/' in the immediate values and did not recurse into dictionaries.
insights.solution: In Manager._build_kwargs, recurse when v is dict, expand when v is str starting with 'env/', and attempt float conversion after expansion when appropriate.
insights.lesson_learned: If configuration expansion is a feature, ensure it’s applied consistently across all nesting levels via recursion, and cover it with unit tests for 1st/2nd/3rd level nesting.

insights.title: Checksum-gated apply workflow for safer sync
insights.problem: Without a checksum gate, a user could apply changes computed from one plan while the underlying planning inputs changed (race/automation mismatch), potentially applying an unintended diff.
insights.root_cause: The existing sync workflow did not provide a strong mechanism to ensure the apply phase corresponds exactly to the prior computed plan.
insights.solution: Add Manager.enable_checksum and Manager.sync(checksum=...). When enable_checksum is active, compute a SHA-256 over a JSON serialization of Plan.data (changes payload). If dry_run is false (or checksum is provided), require checksum to match computed_checksum or raise ManagerException.
insights.lesson_learned: For multi-step workflows (plan then apply), add a content-derived integrity check so apply can only proceed when the operator is applying the exact plan they previously generated. Also ensure the feature is test-covered for both missing and mismatched checksum cases.

tests/test_octodns_manager.py.short_description: Integration-style unit tests validating Manager orchestration, config validation, IDNA, checksum gating, and dump/sync behaviors.
tests/test_octodns_manager.py.description:
This file provides broad coverage for the `octodns.manager.Manager` orchestration layer using YAML configs under `tests/config`. It largely exercises Manager as a black box via `Manager(...).sync()` and `Manager(...).dump()`, asserting on both return values (e.g., number of changes) and specific exception messages for invalid configurations and eligibility filters.

The tests cover several cross-cutting concerns managed by `Manager`: dynamic plugin configuration errors (missing/bad provider classes and plan output classes), correct handling of missing env vars through `EnvironSecrets`, and high-level sync behaviors including sub-zone eligibility, alias zone rules, and compare/dump flows. Two particularly important areas are (1) IDNA handling where eligibility zone names can be passed in utf-8 or IDNA-encoded forms, and (2) checksum gating where applying changes requires a checksum to match computed plan data.

The suite also includes direct unit tests for `_AggregateTarget` behavior using real provider objects from helpers (Simple/Geo/Dynamic/NoSshFp providers). This validates the internal semantics that Manager relies upon for target selection and record support checks.

key_constructs.name: TestManager
key_constructs.purpose: Holds all Manager tests asserting orchestration and validation behaviors.
key_constructs.reasoning: Concentrates coverage for many Manager features, including error message expectations.

key_behaviors: asserts Manager raises ManagerException for various misconfigurations
asserts sync returns expected change counts for specific eligibility settings
asserts IDNA zone eligibility normalization results in specific error messages
asserts checksum gating enforces computed checksum match before apply
asserts dump produces expected output files per zone and supports output_provider indirection
unit-tests _AggregateTarget support semantics and dynamic SUPPORTS_* attribute forwarding

pitfalls.mistake: Changing exception message text in Manager validation paths without updating these tests.
pitfalls.consequence: Many tests use exact `str(ctx.exception)` equality or substring checks; mismatched wording causes failures.
pitfalls.prevention: When editing Manager error handling/wording, run this suite and adjust only if intended.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering IDNA normalization behavior such that utf-8 and IDNA encoded zone keys resolve differently.
pitfalls.consequence: `test_idna_eligible_zones` asserts the zone name included in exception messages; both behavior and message content must match expectations.
pitfalls.prevention: Re-run IDNA-related tests after any change to zone normalization.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing checksum computation or JSON payload semantics used by Manager.enable_checksum.
pitfalls.consequence: Checksum mismatch tests validate both failure modes and message formatting (splitting on '=').
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure checksum gating remains aligned with existing tests for missing/wrong/correct checksum.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying _AggregateTarget SUPPORTS_* behavior to be OR-based instead of AND-based.
pitfalls.consequence: _AggregateTarget tests explicitly check SUPPORTS_GEO and SUPPORTS_DYNAMIC for pairings, and broader sync planning counts will change.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve logical semantics for SUPPORTS_* aggregation.

key_sections: Early tests: missing/bad provider/plan output classes and config problems
test_simple: expected sync change counts under multiple eligibility and include_meta/max_workers configurations
test_enable_checksum: checksum gating behavior and error message format assumptions
test_idna_eligible_zones: IDNA inputs mapping to error messages
test_aliases: alias zone validation scenarios
test_aggregate_target: direct unit assertions for internal _AggregateTarget semantics
test_dump* and output_provider tests: filesystem side effects and error handling
skip_unless_needed: Helper functions at top (get_config_filename/reset) except when adjusting temp-dir/dump expectations

relevant_snippets.description: Configuration/validation tests expecting ManagerException for invalid/missing provider and plan output configs.
Checksum gating: asserts failure without checksum when enable_checksum enabled and success with correct checksum.
IDNA eligibility: asserts specific exception messages when eligible_zones passed in utf-8 or IDNA forms.
Direct `_AggregateTarget` semantics checks for SUPPORTS_* attribute aggregation and supports(record) behavior.
Dump tests asserting unknown source errors, zone='*' expansion, and unknown zone handling producing ManagerException.

related_files.relationship: Defines Manager and internal helpers (like _AggregateTarget) whose behavior is validated here.
related_files.reason_to_check: When changing orchestration/validation/checksum logic, this file will reveal mismatches in counts and exception strings.

related_files.relationship: Covers env secret parsing behaviors that Manager relies on when expanding env/VAR references.
related_files.reason_to_check: If failing Manager env-related tests, check EnvironSecrets type coercion expectations.

insights.title: Added coverage for zone-specific threshold parsing and subzone precedence
insights.problem: Without tests, it would be easy to implement zone thresholds in docs/config but forget to plumb them into the Zone object or get precedence wrong (zone vs subzone).
insights.root_cause: None (test addition).
insights.solution: Added `test_zone_threshold` asserting that `update_pcent_threshold`/`delete_pcent_threshold` are loaded for both a zone and its subzone from `tests/config/zone-threshold.yaml`.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding new config keys with precedence rules, include explicit tests for both top-level zone and nested subzone behavior.

insights.title: Regression tests for Manager input validation and checksum gating
insights.problem: Safety features and validation logic can regress; tests must lock down expected error messages and behavior.
insights.root_cause: Core logic changes happened in Manager.sync and config expansion; without targeted tests, incorrect future changes could silently break guarantees.
insights.solution: Add/adjust tests: test_missing_zone expects ManagerException containing 'Passed zone:'; test_build_kwargs validates nested env expansion; test_enable_checksum verifies (a) no checksum with enable_checksum fails apply, (b) wrong checksum fails, (c) correct checksum allows apply.
insights.lesson_learned: When introducing integrity checks or input validation, assert on both the failure mode and part of the error message; then add a success-path test with computed checksum.

insights.title: Tests updated to use __version__ instead of __VERSION__
insights.problem: Tests and internal version retrieval assertions were tied to legacy constant.
insights.root_cause: Migration to `octodns.__version__` for Pep-8 compliance.
insights.solution: Updated imports and expectations from `__VERSION__` to `__version__` (including dummy module attributes).
insights.lesson_learned: When migrating public API constants, update all consumers (tests, tooling, and mocks) in the same batch to avoid transient failures.

tests/test_octodns_secret_environ.py.short_description: Unit tests for EnvironSecrets fetching env vars with default values and numeric coercion.
tests/test_octodns_secret_environ.py.description:
This file tests `octodns.secret.environ.EnvironSecrets.fetch`, which is the default secret handler that Manager wires in under the name `'env'`. The tests cover: retrieving required env vars as strings, coercing env values to int or float depending on whether the value contains a dot, and raising `EnvironSecretsException` with a specific message when an env var is missing.

It also verifies the “default value” syntax `ENV_VAR/DEFAULT_VALUE`, where the default is used only when the env var is missing. The tests include defaults that contain slashes to ensure the split logic only divides on the first slash. Additional coverage includes coercion of default values into int/float when the default contains numeric representations with/without dots, and the behavior when the env var exists (default ignored).

octodns/processor/arpa.py.short_description: Generates reverse (PTR) records for A/AAAA source records via automatic ARPA mapping.
octodns/processor/arpa.py.description:
This module defines `AutoArpa`, a processor that inspects the desired/source zone’s records and builds an internal mapping (`self._records`) from reverse-lookup ARPA FQDNs (e.g., `4.3.2.1.in-addr.arpa.`) to a list of candidate PTR targets. For each eligible source record, it computes the reverse pointer name for each IP value and stores tuples of `(auto_arpa_priority, record_ttl, fqdn)`.

Eligibility is limited to A/AAAA records, with a special-case for wildcard owner names (`record.name == '*'`). By default, wildcard A/AAAA records are excluded; they are only included when `wildcard_replacement` is provided, in which case `record.fqdn` is materialized by replacing `'*'` with the configured string before PTR generation. The processor also optionally includes geo and dynamic record values: geo expands `record.geo` into additional IPs, and dynamic expands `record.dynamic.pools[*].data['values']` to include each `value['value']` entry. TTL for PTR records is taken from either the source record (`inherit_ttl=True`) or the processor’s configured `ttl`.

The second phase, `populate`, consumes `self._records` and adds PTR records to matching reverse zones. It only creates PTR records when the ARPA name ends with `.{zone.name}` (so boundary matching depends on string suffix logic). It then orders and uniquifies the PTR targets using `_order_and_unique_fqdns`, which sorts by IP target FQDN first, deduplicates by FQDN, and truncates the number of unique FQDNs to `max_auto_arpa`. The TTL set on the generated PTR record is taken from the first tuple in the ordered/uniquified list (i.e., based on the selected target ordering rather than priority).

This file fits into a larger octoDNS processing pipeline where processors transform a desired state before it’s planned/applied; here the transformation is generation of PTR records into reverse zones based on existing A/AAAA values. The unit tests in `tests/test_octodns_processor_arpa.py` define expected behavior for wildcard handling, geo/dynamic expansion, PTR boundary matching, deduplication/ordering, and TTL inheritance.

key_constructs.name: AutoArpa
key_constructs.purpose: Collects reverse-lookup PTR targets from A/AAAA (incl. geo/dynamic) and populates PTR records into reverse zones.
key_constructs.reasoning: The class splits logic into two phases (`process_source_zone` then `populate`) and uses an internal mapping (`self._records`) so that reverse zones can be populated later based on the source zone analysis.

pitfalls.consequence: PTR records will be missing for wildcard A/AAAA when `wildcard_replacement` is set, or incorrectly included when it is not
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the current guard: wildcard owner (`record.name == '*'`) is ignored unless `wildcard_replacement is not None`, and wildcard replacement is applied to `record.fqdn` before PTR mapping

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the suffix matching logic in `populate` (arpa.endswith(f'.{zone_name}'))
pitfalls.consequence: PTR records may be generated in non-matching boundary reverse zones or omitted from matching ones
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the exact suffix rule semantics used in tests, including the computed `n = len(zone_name) + 1` slicing

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying ordering/deduplication behavior in `_order_and_unique_fqdns`
pitfalls.consequence: Generated PTR `values` order and the chosen PTR `ttl` (from the first tuple) will change, breaking tests and potentially user expectations
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the function’s current approach: sort the full `(priority, ttl, fqdn)` tuples, dedupe by `fqdn`, and truncate by count of unique FQDNs

pitfalls.mistake: Misunderstanding TTL selection and how it is applied to the created PTR record
pitfalls.consequence: PTR records may have the wrong TTL (e.g., inheriting source TTL when not requested, or using a different TTL than tests expect)
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure TTL used in the PTR record is sourced from `record.ttl` when `inherit_ttl=True`, else from `self.ttl`, and that `populate` uses `fqdns[0][0]` as the PTR TTL

insights.title: AutoArpa supports wildcard_replacement for '*' A/AAAA records
insights.problem: AutoArpa logic ignored wildcard A/AAAA records by default, preventing generation of PTR records when wildcard IP targets should be mapped.
insights.root_cause: Record type handling filtered out record.name == '*' unless additional configuration was present (previously no replacement fill-in was supported).
insights.solution: Commit eeed1aa adds wildcard_replacement option; when set, it replaces '*' within record.fqdn before computing PTR target and stores fqdn accordingly for ptr generation.
insights.lesson_learned: Wildcard handling is easy to get subtly wrong; if wildcard records should participate, add explicit configuration for how to materialize the wildcard into a concrete FQDN.

tests/test_octodns_processor_arpa.py.short_description: Unit tests for AutoArpa processor PTR generation logic including wildcard/geo/dynamic.
tests/test_octodns_processor_arpa.py.description:
This test module validates the behavior of `AutoArpa` across a range of DNS record scenarios and edge cases. The tests mainly exercise two phases: (1) `process_source_zone` populating `aa._records` with expected reverse ARPA keys and `(priority, ttl, fqdn)` tuples, and (2) `populate` creating PTR `Record` entries in reverse zones when the ARPA keys match the reverse zone name suffix.

The suite covers single and multi-value A records, AAAA to ip6.arpa reverse pointer generation, and expansions for geo and dynamic records. It asserts the internal `_records` mapping for many scenarios, which makes this test file sensitive to tuple ordering, deduplication, and truncation semantics. `test_arpa_priority` focuses specifically on `_order_and_unique_fqdns`, verifying that ordering and deduplication are performed by FQDN and that limiting via `max_auto_arpa` truncates by unique target count.

Several tests validate configuration-driven behavior: `test_arpa_inherit_ttl` checks that PTR TTL becomes the source record TTL when `inherit_ttl=True`, and `test_wildcard` checks wildcard record inclusion is disabled by default but enabled with `wildcard_replacement`. `test_single_value_A_with_space` covers validation/lenient handling by expecting a `ValidationError` when constructing a Record with spaces in the name without `lenient=True`, and then asserting that when lenient is used, the resulting fqdn propagates into PTR generation.

Overall, these tests encode both external outcomes (PTR record creation in matching reverse zones) and internal intermediate state (`aa._records`), so any modifications to `octodns/processor/arpa.py` must preserve the exact semantics asserted here.

octodns/__init__.py.short_description: Defines the octodns package version constants for external consumers.
octodns/__init__.py.description:
This file is the package initializer for the `octodns` Python package. Its primary purpose is to expose a version identifier at import time via module-level constants. That version is commonly used by CLI tooling, logging, packaging/metadata checks, and downstream integrations that want to programmatically assert which octodns version is running.

The file contains a string description (module docstring) and two version constants: `__version__` and `__VERSION__`. Both are assigned the same literal value, making them aliases. The commit insight indicates a transition from the non-standard `__VERSION__` name to the more conventional `__version__`, and the coexistence of both names is intentional to preserve backwards compatibility during the deprecation period.

Because this file is imported implicitly whenever `import octodns` (or submodules through `octodns.*`) occur, edits to these constants can affect many callers at once. Maintaining the alias relationship (same version value) and preserving the exact symbol names are the most important behavioral constraints when modifying this file.

key_constructs.name: __version__
key_constructs.purpose: Public, PEP-8 style version constant intended for external consumers.
key_constructs.reasoning: Downstream code typically imports `octodns.__version__` to display or verify the installed version; keeping its value and alias behavior consistent is critical for compatibility.

key_constructs.name: __VERSION__
key_constructs.purpose: Legacy version constant retained for backwards compatibility.
key_constructs.reasoning: Existing integrations or tests may still reference `octodns.__VERSION__`; removing or desynchronizing it can break those consumers.

key_behaviors: exposes version constants on package import

pitfalls.consequence: Downstream checks that read one name may observe a different version than others, causing inconsistent behavior or failing assertions.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the aliasing relationship so both names always reflect the same version literal during the transition period.

pitfalls.mistake: Renaming or removing `__VERSION__`
pitfalls.consequence: Older tooling/tests that import or parse `__VERSION__` may fail with `AttributeError` or mismatch expectations.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep legacy symbols present while the deprecation window exists, and only remove them when the codebase and ecosystem have fully migrated.
