Ported flattening methods marked pragma: no cover unless diagnosing merge/include behavior

relevant_snippets.description: Asserts safe_load natural ordering of mixed numeric/string-like keys and safe_dump output ordering for record-like keys.
Validates !include behavior including FileNotFoundError message and included structure composition.
Validates YAML merge + !include precedence using tests/config/include/merge.yaml with enforce_order=False.
Validates order_mode parsing/dumping differences and exact InvalidOrder message strings.

related_files.relationship: Direct unit tests exercising safe_load/safe_dump, include, include+merge, and ordering contracts.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavior change in ordering, error messaging, or include/merge semantics must keep these assertions consistent.

insights.title: Rework YAML !include to support YAML merge syntax (<<: !include ...)
insights.problem: Previous !include implementation supported list-merging array syntax but did not integrate cleanly with native YAML merge operator (<<:). Also, it constrained types (dicts vs arrays) and had specialized flatten logic for array merging.
insights.root_cause: The !include custom constructor/flattening logic was designed around an older “array syntax” for merging multiple included files, rather than composing with YAML’s built-in merge semantics.
insights.solution:
Commit 45e372a replaces the prior list/array-based include merging with a new implementation:
- Adds construct_include for single-file include.
- Adds flatten_include to integrate !include with YAML merge (`<<`) by yielding composed values.
- Ports flattener support for seq/map from upstream PyYAML PR patterns (via add_flattener/flatten_yaml_seq/flatten_yaml_map and overriding flatten_mapping).
- Wraps mapping construction with ContextDict to preserve line/column context for errors.
This enables `merged-config: { <<: !include base.yaml, <<: !include overrides.yaml, ... }`.
insights.lesson_learned: When extending YAML parsing, prefer integrating with native YAML merge behavior instead of building parallel merge syntaxes; but ensure you port/maintain flattener logic carefully and keep error context to avoid regressions in diagnostics.

insights.title: Removed/changed legacy !include array syntax coverage (arrays of arrays/dicts) in tests/docs
insights.problem: The batch’s docs/tests indicate the old !include array syntax behavior was no longer maintained; the test suite removed cases for array-of-arrays, array-of-dicts, and empty-array include syntax.
insights.root_cause: Migration to the merge-operator-based include model changed how composite structures are expected to be built from included YAML.
insights.solution: Commit 45e372a updates documentation (docs/include_directive.rst), adjusts tests to remove legacy include array syntax expectations, and adds a new merge.yaml test fixture verifying merge behavior.
insights.lesson_learned: When changing YAML include semantics, treat it as a migration: update docs, update/replace tests for removed features, and consider retaining backward compatibility if feasible or clearly document deprecations.

insights.title: safe_dump/safe_load now support configurable order_mode with explicit error
insights.problem: Yaml ordering enforcement was not customizable beyond the default natural sort behavior; invalid ordering options produced unclear failures.
insights.root_cause: Original loader/dumper selection used fixed loader classes and did not validate order_mode inputs with a domain-specific exception.
insights.solution: Add InvalidOrder exception; refactor loader selection via enforce_order+order_mode; add separate dumper classes (NaturalSortingDumper, SimpleSortingDumper) and dispatch with InvalidOrder on unknown order_mode.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding config knobs (order_mode), validate upfront with a targeted exception and update tests to cover both correct orders and invalid options.

tests/test_octodns_yaml.py.short_description: Unit tests for octodns.yaml safe_load/safe_dump ordering and include/merge behavior.
tests/test_octodns_yaml.py.description:
This test module validates the observable contracts provided by `octodns/yaml.py`. It checks that parsing enforces key order by default (natural mode), that dumped YAML matches exact expected ordering/formatting, and that `!include` works both as a simple include and when combined with YAML merge syntax (`<<`). It also verifies ordering behavior in 'simple' mode and ensures invalid order_mode inputs raise `InvalidOrder` with exact error strings.

The tests are highly assertion-string oriented for `safe_dump()`: they compare full YAML output including the `---` document start marker, quoting of keys, newline placement, and the exact sorted order. For parsing failures caused by out-of-order keys, the test inspects `ctx.exception.problem` and expects a substring that begins with `keys out of order: expected ... got ... at`. This means edits to loader validation must preserve both content and formatting of the error’s `problem` attribute.

Include tests verify two layers: (1) included content structure matches expectations, and (2) missing included files propagate a FileNotFoundError with an exact message format containing the resolved path. The include-merge test reads a fixture YAML (`tests/config/include/merge.yaml`) and loads it with `enforce_order=False`, focusing on merge precedence behavior rather than ordering enforcement.

key_constructs.name: TestYaml
key_constructs.purpose: TestCase container for YAML behavior verification.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides the test entry points covering include behavior, merge behavior, ordering enforcement, and output ordering.

key_constructs.name: test_stuff
key_constructs.purpose: Validates baseline ordering for safe_load and deterministic output for safe_dump.
key_constructs.reasoning: Covers natural sorting with mixed int/string keys and ensures dumping produces canonical order.

key_constructs.name: test_include
key_constructs.purpose: Validates !include functionality and missing include file error propagation.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures include resolution works relative to fixture file locations and error messages remain stable.

key_constructs.name: test_include_merge
key_constructs.purpose: Validates !include combined with YAML merge operator precedence.
key_constructs.reasoning: Confirms the custom flattening logic correctly integrates includes into YAML's native merge semantics.

key_constructs.name: test_order_mode
key_constructs.purpose: Validates order_mode differences ('natural' vs 'simple') and InvalidOrder errors.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures both parse-time enforcement and dump-time ordering respect order_mode and that invalid modes fail fast with exact messages.

key_behaviors: asserts natural ordering on load and dump by default
asserts simple ordering behavior when order_mode='simple'
asserts ConstructorError content for out-of-order keys
asserts !include loads referenced YAML relative to fixture paths
asserts missing !include raises FileNotFoundError with an exact string
asserts YAML merge precedence with !include via fixture merge.yaml

pitfalls.mistake: Relaxing equality assertions on YAML dump output without updating expectations.
pitfalls.consequence: Changes in quoting, ordering, or formatting could go unnoticed or cause failing tests due to exact-match comparisons.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat expected output strings as part of the contract; update only with corresponding, intentional behavior changes and keep quoting/newlines stable.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing exception attributes or message formatting used in assertions.
pitfalls.consequence: Tests will fail even if logical behavior is correct (e.g., if `ConstructorError.problem` text differs).
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve `problem` formatting used by the tests, especially the 'keys out of order...' prefix and ' at' delimiter.

key_sections: test_stuff: baseline ordering + dump string expectations
test_include: include composition + missing-file error message
test_include_merge: merge precedence fixture coverage
test_order_mode: parse-time enforcement in both modes + InvalidOrder exact strings
skip_unless_needed: Small assertions that only confirm specific ordering cases unless diagnosing ordering/dumping behavior

related_files.relationship: Implements the APIs under test: safe_load, safe_dump, ordering enforcement, and include/merge handling.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change in loader/dumper selection, ordering semantics, include resolution, or error message formatting must keep these tests aligned.

insights.title: YAML include tests updated to reflect move from array-syntax includes to merge syntax
insights.problem: Existing include tests validated legacy !include array behaviors (array-of-arrays, array-of-dicts, empty-array => null) which are inconsistent with the new merge-support design.
insights.root_cause: Implementation changed to integrate !include with YAML merge operators, altering supported composition patterns.
insights.solution: Commit 45e372a updates expectations in test_include (dict include result now includes additional keys via dict.yaml) and adds a new test_include_merge reading merge.yaml.
insights.lesson_learned: When the parser semantics change, update tests to assert the new composition model rather than retaining old assumptions; include targeted tests for merge/override precedence.

insights.title: Add/extend tests for order_mode correctness and InvalidOrder handling
insights.problem: Ordering enforcement needed test coverage for both natural and simple modes, including mismatch behavior and invalid order_mode parameters.
insights.root_cause: New functionality introduced conditional loader/dumper paths and a new InvalidOrder exception.
insights.solution: Update tests to assert safe_load/safe_dump output ordering for 'natural' vs 'simple', verify ConstructorError message content when out of order keys occur, and assert InvalidOrder is raised for unknown order_mode values.
insights.lesson_learned: For YAML ordering enforcement, assert not just that an exception occurs, but also the diagnostic content to keep errors actionable.

octodns/record/mx.py.short_description: MX record value parsing/validation with IDNA encoding and templated exchange support.
octodns/record/mx.py.description:
This module defines the MX-specific value object (`MxValue`) and the corresponding record type (`MxRecord`). `MxValue` represents a single MX RDATA item as a small dict-like structure containing `preference` and `exchange`, and provides parsing/validation hooks that the broader record framework can call when ingesting provider data, comparing desired state, and rendering RDATA text.

The module is designed to interoperate with other record value conventions in the codebase: it inherits `EqualityTupleMixin` for consistent equality/hashing semantics based on `_equality_tuple`, and it relies on shared helpers like `unquote()` (for RDATA text parsing) and `validate_target_fqdn()` (for validating the exchange hostname as an FQDN with consistent error formatting). It also applies IDNA encoding (`idna_encode()`) to the exchange value to ensure internationalized domain names are normalized consistently.

A notable behavior is templating: `MxValue.template(params)` only formats the `exchange` when it contains `{` (and a `}` is implicitly expected for `.format(**params)` to succeed). This is intentionally aligned with the shared templating bypass behavior in `validate_target_fqdn()`, which skips FQDN validation when templating braces are present, allowing placeholders to pass through validation until substitution occurs later in the pipeline.

key_constructs.name: MxValue
key_constructs.purpose: Model and validate a single MX (preference, exchange) RDATA value with IDNA normalization and optional templating.
key_constructs.reasoning: MX RDATA is composite (priority/preference + domain), so this class centralizes parsing/validation, equality semantics, and templating behavior for exchange formatting.

key_constructs.name: parse_rdata_text
key_constructs.purpose: Parse a single MX RDATA text string into preference/exchange components.
key_constructs.reasoning: The record framework uses this to convert provider-style string RDATA into structured values; it must tolerate quoted exchange via `unquote()` and handle preference being numeric or left as-is.

key_constructs.name: validate
key_constructs.purpose: Validate a list of MX value dicts, returning human-readable reasons for any errors.
key_constructs.reasoning: Validation is expected to return a list of strings (rather than raise), matching other record validators and enabling aggregated reporting.

key_constructs.name: process
key_constructs.purpose: Convert parsed provider values into `MxValue` instances.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the bridge from raw structured input (dicts) into the normalized value class used for comparisons and rendering.

key_constructs.name: template
key_constructs.purpose: Apply templating parameters to the exchange field when placeholders are present.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures record templating can substitute into exchange hostnames without touching preference, and preserves templated values for later equality/plan stages.

key_constructs.name: MxRecord
key_constructs.purpose: Register the MX record type for the framework.
key_constructs.reasoning: It ties `_type = 'MX'` and `_value_type = MxValue` into the central Record registry so the plan/sync system knows how to parse/validate/render MX records.

key_behaviors: parses MX RDATA text into preference/exchange fields
validates MX value dicts and returns field-specific error messages
encodes exchange using IDNA normalization during value construction
supports templating by formatting `exchange` when `{` is present
registers `MxRecord` with the shared Record registry

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the key passed to `validate_target_fqdn` (e.g., using the wrong key name like 'value' vs 'exchange').
pitfalls.consequence: Error messages will no longer match the expected field names used across tests/users, leading to inconsistent validation output (and likely failing tests).
pitfalls.prevention: When editing validation logic, keep the third argument to `validate_target_fqdn(exchange, _type, 'exchange')` aligned with MX’s YAML/dict field naming expectations.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or altering the templating bypass behavior by validating exchange unconditionally when braces are present.
pitfalls.consequence: Templated values containing `{...}` would fail FQDN validation prematurely, breaking templating-driven workflows.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect that `validate_target_fqdn` intentionally returns early when both `{` and `}` are present; ensure MX continues to delegate to it for exchange validation.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming preference is always present as `preference` rather than accommodating `priority` input.
pitfalls.consequence: Parsing provider inputs that use `priority` (older/inconsistent sources) would fail or produce incorrect values.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the dual-field handling in `__init__` that falls back from `preference` to `priority`.

pitfalls.mistake: Treating `exchange` as already unquoted in all code paths and removing `unquote()` usage from `parse_rdata_text`.
pitfalls.consequence: Quoted exchange values from provider text formats would include quotes in the exchange string, breaking IDNA encoding, validation, and equality.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep `unquote(exchange)` during parsing of RDATA text.

key_sections: MxValue.parse_rdata_text: RDATA text splitting/unquoting behavior
MxValue.validate: error accumulation and delegation to validate_target_fqdn for exchange
MxValue.__init__: preference/priority fallback and exchange/value fallback, plus idna_encode normalization
MxValue.template: exchange-only formatting guarded by '{' presence
MxRecord: ties type 'MX' to MxValue and registers it
skip_unless_needed: Properties/setters (preference/exchange/data/rdata_text) unless changing rendering or equality expectations

related_files.relationship: Provides shared FQDN validation and templating bypass semantics used by MX exchange validation.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to `validate_target_fqdn`’s signature or bypass conditions must remain compatible with MX’s call `validate_target_fqdn(exchange, _type, 'exchange')`.

related_files.relationship: Co-evolution pattern: both SRV and MX delegate target-like hostname validation to `validate_target_fqdn` and implement per-field templating.
related_files.reason_to_check: If modifying templating conventions or validation message formats, check SRV for matching patterns to avoid inconsistent user-facing behavior.

insights.title: Delegate MX exchange validation to shared validate_target_fqdn() with correct field key
insights.problem: MX validation error strings and templating bypass behavior were inconsistent with other record types.
insights.root_cause: MX had bespoke validation logic using `FQDN` directly and didn’t share the standardized error formatting or missing-field naming.
insights.solution: Replaced inline FQDN checks with `validate_target_fqdn(exchange, _type, 'exchange')` (and updated callers to pass correct key names).
insights.lesson_learned: When standardizing validation, update both the logic and the error-message structure so downstream tests/users get consistent feedback.

insights.title: `MxValue.template` formats exchange hostnames only when placeholders exist
insights.problem: Templating placeholders in MX exchange values not supported.
insights.root_cause: Missing template method for MX values.
insights.solution: Implement `template(params)` on `MxValue` to format `exchange` when it contains `{` and return a new instance otherwise.
insights.lesson_learned: For record value templating, pick specific fields likely to contain placeholders (here `exchange`) rather than doing broad formatting of entire composite strings.

octodns/record/target.py.short_description: Shared target/hostname validation and value classes with IDNA handling and templating.
octodns/record/target.py.description:
This module centralizes validation and value-wrapping logic for DNS record “target” fields (hostname-like values), which occur across many record types (e.g., SRV target, MX exchange, HTTPS/SVCB target-like fields). The primary function `validate_target_fqdn(target, _type, key='value')` enforces common rules: it reports missing fields, allows null targets (`.`) for certain RR types, bypasses validation for templated values containing `{...}` placeholders, applies IDNA encoding normalization, validates via `fqdn.FQDN(...).is_valid`, and enforces a trailing dot.

In addition to the validator, the file defines two string subclasses that fit the record framework’s value protocol. `_TargetValue` represents a single hostname; `_TargetsValue` represents a list of hostnames (multi-value records). Both provide `parse_rdata_text`, `validate`, `process`, and `template` methods so that the record system can consistently parse/validate/render template-able hostnames. They also perform IDNA encoding in `__new__`, ensuring that regardless of input encoding, internal equality and rendering use the normalized IDNA form.

These value wrappers and the validator are intended to be reused by other record value classes (like MX exchange and SRV target). A key integration constraint is that templated values must be allowed to pass validation before substitution; this is why `validate_target_fqdn` checks for both `{` and `}` before performing FQDN checks.

key_constructs.name: validate_target_fqdn
key_constructs.purpose: Validate a target hostname with shared rules for null targets, templating bypass, IDNA normalization, and trailing dot requirements.
key_constructs.reasoning: Record types rely on consistent error message formats and consistent handling of templating/null-target edge cases, so this function acts as the single source of those semantics.

key_constructs.name: _TargetValue
key_constructs.purpose: String subclass encapsulating a single IDNA-encoded hostname target with record-framework hooks.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides uniform parse/validate/process/template methods for record types that expect a single hostname-like field.

key_constructs.name: _TargetsValue
key_constructs.purpose: String subclass (representing one target) used as element type for records with multiple hostname targets.
key_constructs.reasoning: Although it is a string subclass, its `validate` and `process` methods are written for multi-value record structures.

key_behaviors: returns a list of human-readable validation reasons instead of raising
allows target '.' as null for selected record types (HTTPS, MX, SVCB, SRV)
bypasses FQDN validation when templating braces `{` and `}` are present
IDNA-encodes targets before FQDN validation and rendering
enforces trailing dot on IDNA-encoded hostname strings

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying the templating bypass condition to check only for '{' or only for '}' separately.
pitfalls.consequence: Partially templated or malformed strings could be validated (failing) or vice versa, creating inconsistent behavior across record types and breaking template validation tests.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the current rule: bypass validation only when both '{' and '}' exist in the same target string.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the list of record types for which `target == '.'` is allowed as a null target.
pitfalls.consequence: Record types that legitimately accept null targets would start producing validation errors, while others might incorrectly allow '.' if added.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat the `if target == '.' and _type in [...]` list as record-type policy; coordinate any changes with MX/SRV/SVCB/HTTPS/SRV call sites and tests.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering IDNA encoding behavior (e.g., removing `idna_encode` from `__new__` or from the validation path).
pitfalls.consequence: Equality comparisons and displayed rdata text could become inconsistent across providers/desired states, and validation might reject inputs that should be accepted after normalization.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep IDNA normalization at both validation-time (`validate_target_fqdn`) and value construction-time (`_TargetValue`/`_TargetsValue`).

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the error message key parameter default from `key='value'` or changing formatting strings.
pitfalls.consequence: Downstream tests and user-facing error messages that assert on these strings may fail or become inconsistent.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the existing f-string patterns like `missing {key}` and `{_type} {key} ...` when editing.

key_sections: validate_target_fqdn: ordering of null-target handling, templating bypass, IDNA encode, FQDN validity, and trailing dot enforcement
_TargetValue: single target wrapper; focus on validate/process/template methods and __new__ IDNA encoding
_TargetsValue: multi-value expectations despite being a string subclass; focus on validate/process semantics and template
skip_unless_needed: The internal `parse_rdata_text` methods (they are pass-through) unless integrating with RDATA text parsing

related_files.relationship: MX delegates exchange validation to `validate_target_fqdn` with `key='exchange'` and expects the templating bypass behavior to allow placeholders.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any modification to validation semantics or error message formatting will directly impact MX validation outcomes.

related_files.relationship: SRV uses the same `validate_target_fqdn` helper for its target field, relying on identical templating and null-target rules.
related_files.reason_to_check: If changing the shared validation policy, confirm SRV expectations remain aligned.

insights.title: Centralize and unify target FQDN validation across record types
insights.problem: Target FQDN validation logic was duplicated across record types and produced inconsistent error messaging and templating/lenience behavior.
insights.root_cause: Multiple record classes performed their own validation and bypass rules.
insights.solution: Introduced/expanded `validate_target_fqdn(target, _type, key='value')` and updated record value validators (MX/SRV/SVCB/…) to call it with the correct YAML field name. Also updated logic for allowing null targets (e.g. `target == '.'`) for specific types and provided missing-key field naming in errors.
insights.lesson_learned: Centralize complex validation in one function to keep error formats consistent and make templating bypass behavior uniform.

insights.title: Introduce `Value.template(params)` protocol on record value objects (targets included)
insights.problem: Templating support was needed across different DNS record value types but lacked a consistent interface.
insights.root_cause: There was no uniform method on record value classes to apply parameter substitutions where values may contain `{...}` placeholders.
insights.solution: Add `template(self, params)` to `_TargetsValue` (and later to other value classes in other commits): if no `{` is present, return `self`; otherwise, return a new instance with `.format(**params)` applied.
insights.lesson_learned: When introducing cross-cutting behavior (like templating), implement a small, consistent protocol on all relevant value classes and short-circuit when no templating markers exist to avoid unnecessary object churn.

octodns/processor/clamp.py.short_description: Processor that clamps record TTLs to a configured min/max range.
octodns/processor/clamp.py.description:
This file defines `TtlClampProcessor`, a `BaseProcessor` implementation used in octoDNS processing pipelines to enforce TTL bounds on desired zone records. It exists to provide deterministic TTL normalization during plan generation/sync planning: any record whose `record.ttl` falls outside `[min_ttl, max_ttl]` is modified in-place to the nearest boundary.

The processor’s configuration is expressed via constructor arguments `min_ttl` and `max_ttl`, with defaults of 300 seconds (5 minutes) and 86400 seconds (24 hours). A dedicated exception type `TTLArgumentException` (subclassing `ProcessorException`) is introduced to fail fast when the configuration is invalid (i.e., when `min_ttl` is not less than or equal to `max_ttl`). Logging is performed during initialization and when a record’s TTL is actually changed, using a logger named after the class.

Within the processing lifecycle, `process_source_zone` is the key entry point: it iterates over `desired.records`, computes a clamped TTL for each record, and updates `record.ttl` only when the clamped value differs from the original. The method signature includes `sources` and `lenient` to match the `BaseProcessor` interface, though `sources`/`lenient` are not used by this processor’s logic.

key_constructs.name: TTLArgumentException
key_constructs.purpose: Signals invalid TTL clamp configuration during processor initialization.
key_constructs.reasoning: Because the processor modifies TTLs across many records, rejecting inverted/invalid ranges at init time prevents subtle downstream behavior that would otherwise appear to “work” while applying an unintended clamp.

key_constructs.name: TtlClampProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Clamps TTL values of all records in a desired zone to a configured range.
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements the octoDNS processor contract by overriding `process_source_zone`, ensuring TTL normalization happens consistently within the pipeline stage that handles desired records derived from sources.

key_constructs.name: process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Applies TTL clamping to every record in the provided `desired` zone.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the behavioral core that mutates the zone’s records in-place, which other parts of the system will later use when computing diffs/plans.

key_behaviors: validates min_ttl/max_ttl relationship at initialization
computes per-record clamped TTL within [min_ttl, max_ttl]
mutates desired zone records in-place when TTL changes
logs initialization parameters and TTL changes per record

pitfalls.mistake: Providing a configuration where min_ttl > max_ttl and expecting a no-op or swapped range.
pitfalls.consequence: Initialization raises `TTLArgumentException`, preventing the processor from being used at all.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure configuration invariants satisfy `min_ttl <= max_ttl` before instantiating the processor.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming `sources` and `lenient` affect behavior for this processor.
pitfalls.consequence: Edits that rely on those parameters will have no effect on clamping results, potentially causing confusion in pipelines where other processors use lenient behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat this processor as applying the same clamp logic regardless of source selection and leniency.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the logger/message formatting away from the established parameterized style without accounting for existing logging conventions.
pitfalls.consequence: Inconsistent log output and potentially less efficient logging; may also break expectations in log-capture tests (if any exist elsewhere).
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the parameterized logging approach used by this file when adjusting log statements.

key_sections: TtlClampProcessor.__init__: where config defaults are set and the min/max invariant is enforced
TtlClampProcessor.process_source_zone: main TTL clamping loop and the exact mutation condition
skip_unless_needed: docstring example config unless you’re validating how the processor is intended to be referenced in YAML

related_files.relationship: Defines the `BaseProcessor` contract (including the expected signature/semantics of `process_source_zone`) and `ProcessorException` base type.
related_files.reason_to_check: If processor method signatures or exception handling patterns differ across processors, they must remain compatible with this processor’s override.

related_files.relationship: Orchestrates processor execution within the overall plan/sync workflow.
related_files.reason_to_check: To understand when and how `process_source_zone` is invoked and how in-place mutations to `desired.records` are consumed.

insights.title: TtlClampProcessor now raises when min_ttl > max_ttl
insights.problem: Invalid TTL clamp configuration could silently produce incorrect clamping behavior (effectively an inverted range).
insights.root_cause: Missing validation of argument logic (min_ttl/max_ttl relationship) in the processor initializer.
insights.solution: Commit 5d9fd7e introduces TTLArgumentException (subclass of ProcessorException) and raises if `not min_ttl <= max_ttl`.
insights.lesson_learned: Validate configuration invariants at processor initialization to fail fast rather than producing subtly wrong record modifications downstream.

insights.title: Use logging formatting style instead of f-strings for log messages
insights.problem: Log message construction used f-strings, which can be less consistent/performance-optimized than parameterized logging and may produce overly-evaluated string formatting.
insights.root_cause: Logging style drift across refactors.
insights.solution: Commit 2488116 updates clamp.py to use getLogger(self.__class__.__name__) and parameterized log calls (`self.log.info('...%s...', ...)`).
insights.lesson_learned: Keep logging consistent across the codebase using parameterized formatting to avoid unnecessary string interpolation and to standardize output.

octodns/cmds/report.py.short_description: CLI command to query DNS resolvers for OctoDNS zone records and emit CSV/JSON reports.
octodns/cmds/report.py.description:
This file implements the `octodns-report`-style command (entry via `main()`) that inspects a configured OctoDNS zone, populates it from one or more configured sources, then performs live DNS lookups against one or more user-provided resolver targets. The output summarizes, per record and per resolver, what answers are returned and whether those answers are consistent across all resolvers.

The command is built around `asyncio` concurrency. It constructs a list of async tasks for every combination of `zone.records` and requested resolver, with a `Semaphore(concurrency)` limit to cap in-flight queries. DNS querying is performed with `dns.asyncresolver.Resolver` (configured per query task via `AsyncResolver(configure=False)`), setting `lifetime` and the single target nameserver in `r.nameservers`. Errors are normalized into sentinel strings in `async_resolve` so reporting can proceed even when records are missing or queries fail.

Once all tasks complete, results are aggregated into a nested `queries` structure mapping `record -> resolver -> list-of-answers`. The CSV path writes a header containing one column per resolver, then a row per record including a “Consistent” boolean computed by building a case-insensitive key from the joined answer list. The JSON path builds a nested JSON object indexed by `record.decoded_fqdn` / `record._type` / resolver, storing the answer list for each resolver, plus a `consistent` field derived similarly (case-insensitive). There is special handling for TXT records in the JSON output: it strips surrounding double quotes from each returned string to prevent downstream double-escaping in JSON.

This command fits into the octoDNS system by relying on `octodns.manager.Manager` to load configuration, resolve configured providers/sources, and obtain a zone object containing `zone.records`. It then uses each source’s `populate()` method to fill that zone with current/desired record data before running the network queries. Editors should treat the record objects’ attributes (`decoded_fqdn`, `fqdn`, `_type`, `ttl`) and their ordering behavior (zone record iteration order, plus explicit sorting of `zone.records` and `queries.items`) as part of the command’s stable behavior expectations.

key_constructs.name: async_resolve
key_constructs.purpose: Asynchronously resolve a single OctoDNS record against one DNS resolver with concurrency limiting and normalized error sentinels.
key_constructs.reasoning: The reporting semantics (what strings represent NXDOMAIN/timeout/no answer) and concurrency behavior live here. Any changes must preserve the returned tuple shape `[record, resolver, answer]` and the sentinel normalization used later for consistency checks and output rendering.

key_constructs.name: main
key_constructs.purpose: Parse CLI args, load manager/configured sources, build resolver list, run concurrent async DNS queries, and print CSV or JSON report.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the orchestration entry point: it defines user-facing CLI options, determines resolver de-duplication, schedules tasks, aggregates results, and chooses the CSV/JSON formatting and consistency logic.

key_behaviors: Loads a configured zone from one or more OctoDNS providers/sources via Manager
Normalizes resolver input: accepts IP literals (IPv4/IPv6) or resolves hostnames via A then AAAA
Performs async DNS queries for every (record, resolver) combination with concurrency capped by a semaphore
Normalizes DNS failures into sentinel answer strings to keep output consistent
Aggregates results per record/resolver and computes cross-resolver consistency via case-insensitive keys
Emits either CSV with one column per resolver or JSON with nested record/type/resolver structure

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the sentinel strings emitted for DNS exceptions in `async_resolve`
pitfalls.consequence: The consistency calculation and output rows/columns will change semantics, potentially making previously consistent records appear inconsistent (or vice versa).
pitfalls.prevention: If modifying exception handling, ensure the strings returned still match the logic used later for consistency keys and output formatting.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering resolver de-duplication or IP/hostname normalization rules
pitfalls.consequence: Different resolvers may collapse unexpectedly (or not), changing which columns/JSON entries exist and the computed consistency results.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing flow: resolve hostnames by trying A then AAAA, compress IPv6 via `ip_address(...).compressed`, and only append resolvers not already present.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing JSON TXT normalization without accounting for JSON escaping expectations
pitfalls.consequence: TXT values may become double-escaped or mismatched, changing the effective answer lists and consistency fields for TXT records.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the current behavior of stripping surrounding double quotes for each TXT string in JSON output (only in the JSON path).

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying consistency computation input without matching CSV vs JSON differences
pitfalls.consequence: CSV and JSON could diverge in how they normalize answers (CSV joins strings, JSON strips quotes then joins).
pitfalls.prevention: Be cautious: CSV uses `answer = ' '.join(answers.get(resolver, []))` then `answer.lower()` as the consistency key; JSON uses normalized list values and `len(values_check)==1` based on `' '.join(answer).lower()`.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming task ordering will be deterministic and using it implicitly
pitfalls.consequence: While `queries` is aggregated by record/resolver keys, the order of records and resolvers in outputs is determined by sorting (`sorted(zone.records)`, `sorted(queries.items())`) and insertion order of resolvers. Changing this can change output ordering relied upon by users/tests.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the current ordering mechanisms: record sorting via `sorted(zone.records)` and stable resolver order based on discovery and de-duplication.

key_sections: main: argument parsing + Manager/zone/source population
main: resolver normalization (IP literals vs hostname resolution, A then AAAA) and de-duplication
async_resolve: per-query concurrency limiting and exception-to-sentinel mapping
main: task scheduling, result aggregation into `queries`, and CSV/JSON output + consistency computation
skip_unless_needed: The import section unless adding/removing dependencies (dns.asyncresolver, csv/json/asyncio utilities)

related_files.relationship: Provides `Manager(args.config_file)`, `manager.providers[...]`, and `manager.get_zone(zone_name)` used to load zones and sources.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any changes in how providers/sources are exposed or how zones/records are structured may affect `report.py` expectations about record attributes and iteration.

related_files.relationship: Defines `ArgumentParser` used to build the CLI interface.
related_files.reason_to_check: If CLI argument behavior or parsing conventions change, it can affect required options and default types consumed by `report.py`.

insights.title: Fix TXT record value escaping by stripping surrounding quotes
insights.problem: TXT record strings were being unnecessarily escaped (double-quoted) by downstream JSON handling, likely due to query/serialization producing quotes that the JSON module re-escaped.
insights.root_cause: The reporter stored TXT answers including quote characters, then JSON-dumped them, causing extra escaping beyond what OctoDNS users expected.
insights.solution: Commit 8f8e819 updates TXT answer handling in octodns/cmds/report.py to strip surrounding double quotes: `answer = [a.strip('"') for a in answers.get(resolver)]`.
insights.lesson_learned: When serializing DNS TXT content, normalize whether quotes are part of the semantic value vs formatting artifact; apply consistent normalization before JSON/CSV output to prevent double escaping.

insights.title: Rewrite octodns-report to use async DNS queries + concurrency limiting; add IPv6 resolver support
insights.problem: The old report implementation likely relied on threaded resolver calls and limited resolver types (IPv4/hostname) and output formats.
insights.root_cause: Concurrency and resolver capabilities were constrained by the previous use of dns.resolver.Resolver wrapped in a ThreadPoolExecutor; also lacked built-in IPv6 resolver input normalization.
insights.solution:
Commit 07b7521 rewrites report:
- Uses asyncio + dns.asyncresolver.
- Adds --concurrency and semaphore limit.
- Supports IPv6 literals using ipaddress.ip_address().compressed.
- Resolves non-IP resolver inputs as hostnames by trying A then AAAA.
- Produces csv/json outputs.
insights.lesson_learned: For IO-heavy commands, async + explicit concurrency caps can improve throughput and responsiveness; when supporting resolver IPs, normalize IPv6 and validate resolver inputs before querying.

insights.title: Deterministic sorting removed from async_resolve return (consistency now relies on stored answer order)
insights.problem: The earlier version sorted DNS answers to avoid order sensitivity; the rewritten version returns answers in sorted(...) form then later uses joined answers for consistency, but the subsequent logic changed around it.
insights.root_cause: Complexity in the rewrite: answer list ordering and later normalization for consistency checks can be easy to break when moving from streaming output to batch JSON/CSV aggregation.
insights.solution: In commit 8f8e819, async_resolve now returns `answer` (not sorted) but JSON/csv formatting uses joins and a lowercase normalized key; the prior behavior was adjusted as part of TXT escaping fix.
insights.lesson_learned: When refactoring reporting logic, preserve ordering/normalization semantics used for “consistency” calculations; add tests for stability across record ordering changes and TXT quoting differences.

octodns/record/naptr.py.short_description: NAPTR record value parsing/validation, rendering to RFC text, and templating.
octodns/record/naptr.py.description:
This module defines the NAPTR (Naming Authority Pointer) record’s value model and the record type wrapper used by octodns’ generic record framework. The core abstraction is `NaptrValue`, a dict-like container that also participates in octodns’ equality semantics via `EqualityTupleMixin`. It is responsible for parsing provider/zone text (RDATA), validating the structured value representation, normalizing and storing fields in a consistent internal format, and rendering back to canonical RDATA text.

`NaptrRecord` wires the value model into the rest of the system by inheriting from `ValuesMixin` and `Record`, setting `_type = 'NAPTR'`, and `_value_type = NaptrValue`. This registration allows the framework (parsers, diff/equality, plan/apply pipelines) to interpret NAPTR records consistently alongside other RR types.

Notable behaviors include: (1) `parse_rdata_text` expects RDATA tokens separated by spaces in the order `order preference flags service regexp replacement`, with RFC-3403 requiring quoting for the first three “character-string” fields; this code removes quotes via `unquote` for those fields and leaves the replacement token unquoted. (2) Flags are normalized to uppercase in `__init__`, and validation accepts both uppercase and lowercase variants because `VALID_FLAGS` includes lowercase entries; this helps prevent persistent diffs due to providers changing case. (3) `template(params)` only applies string formatting when any of `service`, `regexp`, or `replacement` contain `{`, and it returns a new `NaptrValue` instance with only those fields formatted.

key_constructs.name: NaptrValue
key_constructs.purpose: Represents one NAPTR RDATA value with parsing, validation, canonical rendering, and templating.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the canonical structured representation used for equality/diffing and for converting between provider text and octodns internal state; edits must preserve parsing/rendering consistency and normalization.

key_constructs.name: parse_rdata_text
key_constructs.purpose: Parses an RDATA text line fragment into a dict with typed/nonnormalized components (order/preference as ints when possible, other fields via `unquote`).
key_constructs.reasoning: Providers and zone file readers likely call this when ingesting records; it must match `rdata_text` output structure to avoid round-trip drift.

callers.context: NaptrValue.parse_rdata_text('one two three four five six'),

key_constructs.name: validate
key_constructs.purpose: Validates each value dict in `data` and returns a list of human-readable reasons for invalid fields.
key_constructs.reasoning: The framework likely uses the returned reasons to surface config/provider errors; adding/removing checks can change error messaging and validation strictness.

key_constructs.name: process
key_constructs.purpose: Converts a list of structured dicts into `NaptrValue` instances.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures downstream code always deals with `NaptrValue` objects so equality, templating, and rendering work uniformly.

key_constructs.name: __init__
key_constructs.purpose: Normalizes types (casts order/preference to int) and canonicalizes flags to uppercase while storing other fields verbatim.
key_constructs.reasoning: Canonicalization is critical for stable diffs; especially `flags.upper()` matching `rdata_text` quoting and `__repr__` output.

key_constructs.name: rdata_text
key_constructs.purpose: Renders the value to canonical NAPTR RDATA text, quoting flags/service/regexp per RFC 3403.
key_constructs.reasoning: Must produce the exact token/quoting format expected by zone file writers and internal comparison logic.

key_constructs.name: template
key_constructs.purpose: Formats `{...}` placeholders in `service`, `regexp`, and `replacement` (if any placeholders exist).
key_constructs.reasoning: Templating is used by octodns to produce environment-specific outputs; it must preserve the correct quoting/structure and only apply when needed.

key_constructs.name: NaptrRecord
key_constructs.purpose: Record wrapper for NAPTR that binds `_type` and `_value_type` into the framework.
key_constructs.reasoning: This enables registration and type-based handling elsewhere (parsers, plan/diff, and record writers).

key_behaviors: parses RDATA tokens separated by spaces into typed fields
validates required keys and flag membership against VALID_FLAGS
normalizes internal representation by casting order/preference to int and uppercasing flags
renders canonical RDATA text with quoted flags/service/regexp and unquoted replacement
templates service/regexp/replacement fields when placeholders are present

pitfalls.mistake: Changing tokenization/quoting assumptions between `parse_rdata_text` and `rdata_text`
pitfalls.consequence: Round-trip parsing and rendering can drift, causing persistent diffs or parse failures for valid provider/zone text.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain consistent token order and RFC 3403 quoting for flags/service/regexp while ensuring `replacement` handling matches existing expectations.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or altering flags normalization/acceptance behavior without understanding diff stability
pitfalls.consequence: Provider case changes (e.g., lowercase vs uppercase flags) can produce perpetual plan diffs and unnecessary updates.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve canonical internal form (`value['flags'].upper()`) and the case-insensitive acceptance implied by `VALID_FLAGS`.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying `validate` return structure/wording or removing checks for required keys
pitfalls.consequence: Errors surfaced to users/providers may change, and downstream tests that assert messages may fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep validation output compatible with the framework’s expectations (list of strings reasons).

pitfalls.mistake: Applying templating to the whole value or formatting fields that may not contain placeholders
pitfalls.consequence: Formatting can raise KeyError/ValueError if placeholders don’t exist in params; it can also alter values unexpectedly.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the current placeholder detection: it only formats when `{` appears in service/regexp/replacement.

key_sections: NaptrValue.parse_rdata_text: how provider/zone text is split, unquoted, and typed
NaptrValue.validate: required keys and flag membership enforcement
NaptrValue.__init__: canonical normalization (int casting and flags.upper())
NaptrValue.rdata_text: exact canonical formatting and quoting rules
NaptrValue.template: conditional placeholder formatting and how the new object is created
skip_unless_needed: NaptrValue.__repr__ and __hash__ (only relevant if debugging equality/hash behavior)

relevant_snippets.description: Test(s) exercising NAPTR flags handling, especially case-insensitive input and canonical uppercase output.

related_files.relationship: Unit tests for NaptrValue/naptr record parsing, validation, templating, and equality/diff stability.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change affecting parsing/rendering, validation reasons, or flags normalization can break assertions in these tests.

insights.title: Make NAPTR flags case-insensitive while outputting uppercase
insights.problem: NAPTR flags accepted only one case and could cause persistent diffs if providers changed the case.
insights.root_cause: Validation treated flags literally without normalizing input case, while output formatting preserved input case.
insights.solution: Expanded valid flags set to include lowercase variants and normalized stored flags via `value['flags'].upper()` to keep outputs stable.
insights.lesson_learned: When RFCs define case-insensitivity, validate case-insensitively but normalize internal representation to a canonical format.

insights.title: `NaptrValue.template` formats multiple fields (service/regexp/replacement) if needed
insights.problem: NAPTR templating placeholders not supported.
insights.root_cause: Missing per-field template logic for a composite DNS record value.
insights.solution: Implement `template(params)` that checks whether any relevant fields contain `{` and then formats `service`, `regexp`, and `replacement` on a new instance.
insights.lesson_learned: For composite record values, template only the fields that are expected to contain placeholders, and apply them consistently in one call.

octodns/provider/base.py.short_description: Abstract provider base implementing provider-specific desired/existing processing and plan workflow
octodns/provider/base.py.description:
This file defines `BaseProvider`, the abstract foundation for all octoDNS providers. Providers are both sources of existing DNS state (via `populate`) and targets for applying changes (via an internal `_apply(plan)` implementation). The `plan()` method orchestrates the standard sync lifecycle: create an `existing` Zone, populate it from the provider backend, make a shallow copy of the `desired` Zone, allow provider hooks to normalize/strip unsupported records, run any configured processors, compute the record-level diff via `existing.changes(desired, self)`, and finally build a `Plan` (or `None` when there are no record changes and no meta changes).

A key responsibility of this base class is to handle “unsupported feature” behavior in a provider-configurable way. The `_process_desired_zone` hook is where provider-specific limitations are applied to desired state (including handling for record type support, dynamic record capabilities, multi-value PTR support, and root NS record support). It uses `supports_warn_or_except` to either raise `SupportsException` when `strict_supports=True` or log warnings when `strict_supports=False`. The `root_ns_warnings` toggle specifically gates warning emission about root NS situations without changing the underlying correctness behavior around removal/raising.

This class also acts as a compatibility layer for plugin evolution: processors (and older processors) may not accept a `lenient` kwarg on certain hooks. `BaseProvider.plan()` explicitly tries calling processor methods with `lenient=lenient` and catches `TypeError` when the message indicates “unexpected keyword argument 'lenient'”, falling back to calling without `lenient` while emitting a deprecation warning. Tests in `tests/test_octodns_provider_base.py` heavily exercise these orchestration details, including ordering of operations, root NS behavior, processor lenient propagation/fallback, inclusion filtering via `_include_change`, and meta contribution via `_plan_meta`.

Although the snippet above truncates after the change computation/filtering, the surrounding structure (visible in tests and the docstring) implies it continues to incorporate `_extra_changes` and `_plan_meta`, creates a `Plan`/`UnsafePlan`, and supports `apply()` via an internal `_apply(plan)` method. Any edits to this file must preserve those semantics because tests assert on exact behaviors (e.g., plan presence/absence, counts of changes, warning/exception interactions, and processor calling order).

key_constructs.name: BaseProvider
key_constructs.purpose: Abstract provider base coordinating provider lifecycle steps and extensibility hooks
key_constructs.reasoning: Most provider behavior in octoDNS is centralized here: it defines the orchestration of populate/process/processor/diff/plan/apply and establishes compatibility behavior (e.g., lenient propagation fallbacks) that third-party plugins rely on.

key_constructs.name: _process_desired_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Normalize/strip unsupported records from desired state before planning
key_constructs.reasoning: This method implements the provider limitation handling contract: it may remove or copy/modify records, must warn-or-except via `supports_warn_or_except`, and enforces policy for unsupported record types, dynamic limitations, multi-value PTR limitations, and root NS support.

key_constructs.name: _process_existing_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Normalize/strip unsupported records from existing state before planning
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to reconcile provider backend idiosyncrasies with desired state expectations. Notably, this base implementation handles existing root NS records when root NS is unsupported or not configured in desired.

key_constructs.name: _include_change
key_constructs.purpose: Filter false-positive record changes before they enter the plan
key_constructs.reasoning: Providers can suppress changes that are artifacts of backend behavior (e.g., minimum TTL enforcement). Tests validate that returning False results in no plan.

key_constructs.name: _extra_changes
key_constructs.purpose: Add provider-specific additional changes to the plan beyond record diffs
key_constructs.reasoning: Creates an extension point for plan augmentation that is not purely derived from the record-level diff (e.g., ancillary zone configuration changes).

key_constructs.name: _plan_meta
key_constructs.purpose: Provide plan meta (zone-level non-record change information)
key_constructs.reasoning: Tests assert that when `_plan_meta` returns a non-None value, a plan is produced even if there are zero record changes.

key_constructs.name: supports_warn_or_except
key_constructs.purpose: Implement strict_supports gate for unsupported feature handling
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the semantics used by `_process_desired_zone` when encountering unsupported features; must keep exception messages stable enough for tests and user expectations.

key_constructs.name: plan
key_constructs.purpose: Main orchestration method computing a provider plan of changes from desired state
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the core lifecycle coordinator. Tests assert on operation order (`populate` -> `_process_desired_zone` -> `_process_existing_zone`), lenient parameter passing/fallback into processor hooks, meta behavior, and plan existence conditions.

key_behaviors: populates an existing Zone from the provider backend
copies desired Zone and applies provider normalization in _process_desired_zone
normalizes existing Zone in _process_existing_zone before diffing
runs processors in two phases (process_target_zone, then process_source_and_target_zones)
computes record-level diffs via existing.changes(desired, provider)
filters record changes via _include_change
adds additional provider changes via _extra_changes (extension point)
attaches zone-level meta via _plan_meta (causes Plan creation even without record changes)
creates Plan (or None/falsey) when there are no changes unless meta exists
applies lenient kwarg to processor hooks with backwards-compatible TypeError fallback

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the signature or behavior of `plan()` such that it stops passing `lenient` to processor hooks
pitfalls.consequence: Breaks lenient behavior propagation and fails tests expecting processors to record `called_lenient` as True/False.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the current call/try-catch fallback pattern for lenient across both processor hook phases.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying root NS warning/exception behavior without respecting `root_ns_warnings` and `strict_supports`
pitfalls.consequence: Tests validating warning/no-warning and exception raising for specific combinations will fail; user-facing log semantics change unexpectedly.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the gating: root NS unsupported removal/raise is controlled by `strict_supports`, while warning emission is additionally controlled by `root_ns_warnings`.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering unsupported-feature handling to modify records directly instead of using record copies and `Zone.add_record(..., replace=True)`
pitfalls.consequence: May cause incorrect diffing, shared-state corruption across zones/targets, or inconsistent plan results.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the documented contract: desired/existing zones are shallow copies; record modifications should be copy-based before re-adding with replacement.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing error handling for processors when `lenient` is not accepted
pitfalls.consequence: Older processors/provided plugins will crash with TypeError rather than falling back; tests assert graceful fallback and deprecation signaling for the lenient kwarg mismatch case.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the TypeError message check logic that distinguishes unexpected lenient kwarg from other TypeErrors.

key_sections: BaseProvider.__init__: stores apply_disabled and thresholds, plus strict_supports/root_ns_warnings flags
_process_desired_zone: unsupported record stripping/copying policy and dynamic/PTR/root NS handling
_process_existing_zone: existing-side root NS removal when desired doesn’t configure it or provider can’t manage it
plan: orchestration of populate -> process desired/existing -> processors (two phases) -> diff -> _include_change -> Plan/meta/extra change incorporation
supports_warn_or_except: strict_supports controls raising SupportsException vs logging warnings
skip_unless_needed: _include_change/_extra_changes/_plan_meta: only needed if editing provider extension semantics

relevant_snippets.description: Defines HelperProvider and TrickyProcessor used to exercise plan orchestration, filtering, and apply behavior
Asserts base class abstractness and populate/plan/apply contract; verifies lenient default and SUPPORTS_* requirements
Tests plan/meta behavior and processor lenient kwarg passing/fallback behavior
Tests include_change filtering, processor ordering effects, and root NS behavior

related_files.relationship: Unit tests assert the orchestration and warning/exception semantics of BaseProvider.plan and processing hooks.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral change to planning order, processor invocation, root NS gating, or meta handling will likely require updating these tests or preserving existing semantics.

related_files.relationship: Defines BaseProcessor hook method signatures that BaseProvider.plan invokes with lenient kwarg when supported.
related_files.reason_to_check: If processor hook signatures or compatibility behavior change, BaseProvider.plan’s TypeError fallback logic depends on the exact mismatch error message.

related_files.relationship: Provides Plan.MAX_SAFE_UPDATE_PCENT/MAX_SAFE_DELETE_PCENT and Plan/UnsafePlan semantics created by BaseProvider.plan.
related_files.reason_to_check: Plan creation semantics (when Plan is truthy, handling meta-only plans, and unsafe thresholds) must remain consistent with BaseProvider.plan behavior expected by tests.

insights.title: Propagate lenient mode through provider existing-zone processing
insights.problem: Lenient validation/record handling was not consistently threaded into the provider’s _process_existing_zone flow.
insights.root_cause: The provider base changed its internal processing to support lenient operations, but the existing-zone hook originally had no lenient parameter.
insights.solution: Added a `lenient=False` parameter to `_process_existing_zone(existing, desired, lenient=False)` and ensured `plan()` passes `lenient` through to `_process_existing_zone(..., lenient=lenient)`.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding a global behavior flag like `lenient`, ensure it propagates through all stages where records are created/modified (sources, processors, existing-zone processing).

insights.title: Pass lenient to processor hooks during provider planning with backwards-compatible fallbacks
insights.problem: Processor and target/provider plan hooks may not accept a `lenient` kwarg; calling them with `lenient` could break older implementations.
insights.root_cause: Evolving method signatures (adding `lenient`) introduced `TypeError` risk for third-party/custom processors/providers that weren’t updated.
insights.solution: Updated `BaseProvider.plan()` and processor invocation paths to try calling hooks with `lenient=lenient`, and on `TypeError` with an 'unexpected keyword argument lenient' message, emit a deprecation warning and fall back to calling without lenient.
insights.lesson_learned: For extensible plugin architectures, use signature-detection/fallback strategies and targeted TypeError handling to avoid breaking external implementations.

insights.title: Add root_ns_warnings toggle for root NS record support diagnostics
insights.problem: Providers would warn (or potentially remove root NS records) when `SUPPORTS_ROOT_NS` was true but the desired zone had no root NS record, and similarly when root NS records existed but were not supported; users needed a way to disable these root NS related warnings.
insights.root_cause: Diagnostic logging for root NS handling was unconditional based on `SUPPORTS_ROOT_NS` and `strict_supports` only; there was no knob to suppress warnings while still performing the required cleanup/removal behavior.
insights.solution: Introduced `root_ns_warnings` parameter (default `True`) stored on `BaseProvider`. Warning logs now only emit when `root_ns_warnings` is enabled. When `strict_supports` is enabled, root NS unsupported situations raise `SupportsException` regardless of `root_ns_warnings`.
insights.lesson_learned: For warning-heavy validation/diff-cleanup logic, provide explicit configuration flags to control operator-facing logging without changing underlying correctness behavior.

insights.title: Provider plan now supports meta changes via Plan.meta
insights.problem: Providers previously could only contribute record-specific Create/Update/Delete changes; zone-level non-record changes had no standard mechanism in the plan output.
insights.root_cause: BaseProvider lacked a hook for providers to describe non-record plan requirements; Plan creation only accounted for record changes.
insights.solution: Add BaseProvider._plan_meta(existing, desired, changes) defaulting to None; incorporate the returned meta into Plan(...) and include meta when either record changes or meta exist.
insights.lesson_learned: When extending planning semantics, introduce explicit extension points (_plan_meta) and propagate them through Plan serialization and log/renderers to keep downstream consumers consistent.

octodns/processor/filter.py.short_description: Zone/plan processors to allow/reject records by type, name, value, network, or NS rules.
octodns/processor/filter.py.description:
This module defines a set of processor classes used in octoDNS’ processing pipeline to remove (or retain) records during source/target zone processing and, in one case, to filter plan changes. The processors are primarily “record list filters”: they iterate over `zone.records` and decide whether to call `matches(...)` (keep the record) or `doesnt_match(...)` (remove the record), mutating the provided Zone instance.

A key shared design is `_FilterProcessor` and the `include_target` option. For many filters, the processor can behave differently when called as a source-zone processor versus a target-zone processor: when `include_target` is false, `process_target_zone` returns the zone unchanged, effectively preventing the filter from deleting existing records from the target while still filtering what is read from the source. Several filter families (type/name/value) are implemented by combining a base “_BaseFilter” that implements `_process(...)` with either `AllowsMixin` (keeps matching records) or `RejectsMixin` (removes matching records). For pattern inputs, name/value filters treat list entries starting with `/` as regex patterns (compiled via `re_compile(pattern[1:-1])`), while other entries are treated as exact string matches.

There are also specialized filters: `_NetworkValueBaseFilter` targets only A/AAAA records and uses `ipaddress` to evaluate whether record values fall within configured CIDR networks; `IgnoreRootNsFilter` removes root NS records from the zone; and `ExcludeRootNsChanges` operates on a Plan by either raising a `ValidationError` or removing root NS changes from the plan’s change list depending on its `error` configuration. This module is exercised by unit tests in `tests/test_octodns_processor_filter.py`, which assert record inclusion/exclusion results and expected behavior for `include_target` and plan-change filtering.

key_constructs.name: _FilterProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Base for filters that optionally apply to target zones via include_target.
key_constructs.reasoning: Any edits to shared source/target behavior must preserve how `process_source_zone` delegates to `_process` and how `process_target_zone` conditionally calls `_process` or returns `existing` unchanged.

key_constructs.name: AllowsMixin
key_constructs.purpose: Defines does-not-match behavior as removing non-matching records.
key_constructs.reasoning: The mixin intentionally leaves `matches(...)` unimplemented so that subclasses/mixins decide keep vs remove semantics solely through `doesnt_match`.

key_constructs.name: RejectsMixin
key_constructs.purpose: Defines matches behavior as removing matching records.
key_constructs.reasoning: Reject filters invert the decision: matching records are removed; non-matching records remain.

key_constructs.name: _TypeBaseFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Iterates records and filters based on record._type membership in a configured list.
key_constructs.reasoning: Type filters rely on exact `record._type` string comparisons and mutate the zone while iterating over `zone.records`.

key_constructs.name: TypeAllowlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Keep only records whose types are present in allowlist; others removed.
key_constructs.reasoning: This class wires allowlist inputs into `_TypeBaseFilter` with `AllowsMixin` semantics and supports include_target via `_FilterProcessor`.

key_constructs.name: TypeRejectlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Remove records whose types are present in rejectlist; others kept.
key_constructs.reasoning: This class wires rejectlist inputs into `_TypeBaseFilter` with `RejectsMixin` semantics.

key_constructs.name: _NameBaseFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Iterates records and filters based on record.name against exact and regex patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: Name filters split input list items into an exact-match set and compiled regex list; ordering and match logic must remain consistent with tests.

key_constructs.name: NameAllowlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Keep only records with names matching configured exact/regex allowlist patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: Uses `_NameBaseFilter` + `AllowsMixin`, so only matching names survive and non-matching are removed.

key_constructs.name: NameRejectlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Remove records whose names match configured exact/regex rejectlist patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: Uses `_NameBaseFilter` + `RejectsMixin`, so matching names are removed while non-matching remain.

key_constructs.name: _ValueBaseFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Iterates records and filters based on record.rdata_text values (multi-value aware).
key_constructs.reasoning: Value filters must handle both records with `values` (multi-record style) and `record.value` (single value), and then apply exact/regex matching to the extracted rdata_text list.

key_constructs.name: ValueAllowlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Keep only records whose extracted rdata_text values match configured allowlist patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: Initializes an instance-specific logger in __init__ and relies on `_ValueBaseFilter` for the matching/extraction behavior.

key_constructs.name: ValueRejectlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Remove records whose extracted rdata_text values match configured rejectlist patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: Initializes an instance-specific logger in __init__ and relies on `_ValueBaseFilter` for extraction and matching before applying `RejectsMixin` semantics.

key_constructs.name: _NetworkValueBaseFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Filter A/AAAA records by whether their IP values fall within configured CIDR networks.
key_constructs.reasoning: Uses `ip_network` parsing at construction time and `ip_address` + `product` membership checks per record; constructor raises ValueError on invalid CIDR strings.

key_constructs.name: NetworkValueAllowlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Keep only A/AAAA records whose IP values fall within allowlist CIDRs.
key_constructs.reasoning: Pairs `_NetworkValueBaseFilter` with `AllowsMixin`, so non-matching A/AAAA are removed while other record types are left unchanged by `_NetworkValueBaseFilter`.

key_constructs.name: NetworkValueRejectlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Remove A/AAAA records whose IP values fall within rejectlist CIDRs.
key_constructs.reasoning: Pairs `_NetworkValueBaseFilter` with `RejectsMixin`, so matching A/AAAA are removed while other record types are left unchanged.

key_constructs.name: IgnoreRootNsFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Remove root NS records (type NS with empty name) from the zone.
key_constructs.reasoning: Hard-codes NS root detection using `record._type == 'NS' and not record.name` and mutates the zone records.

key_constructs.name: ExcludeRootNsChanges
key_constructs.purpose: Validate or suppress NS root (empty-name) changes in a Plan.
key_constructs.reasoning: Inspects `plan.changes`, checks each `change.record` for root NS (`record._type == 'NS' and record.name == ''`), then either raises `ValidationError` or removes the change from the plan.

key_constructs.name: ZoneNameFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Filter or error on record names that contain the zone name (definition continues after truncation).
key_constructs.reasoning: The presence of this class (and its tests) implies additional logic exists beyond the truncated portion; edits must preserve expected semantics tested in `TestZoneNameFilter`.

key_behaviors: Mutates zone.records in-place by removing records that do not match criteria
Applies filtering differently for source vs target via include_target
Supports exact string and /regex/ inputs for name/value filters
Extracts rdata_text from record.values (multi) or record.value (single) for value filters
Parses CIDR strings for network-based filtering and validates config eagerly
Removes root NS records from zones or filters root NS changes from plans

pitfalls.mistake: Changing iteration/removal semantics while mutating `zone.records` during a for-loop
pitfalls.consequence: Can cause skipped records or incorrect filtering results depending on Zone.records implementation.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the current remove-via-mixin approach and ensure tests that count/collect remaining record names still pass.

pitfalls.mistake: Breaking regex parsing expectations for pattern strings beginning with '/'
pitfalls.consequence: Patterns may compile incorrectly (e.g., stripping wrong delimiters) leading to mismatched filters.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain `re_compile(pattern[1:-1])` behavior and consistent matching logic for names/values.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering how `_ValueBaseFilter` extracts values from records with `values` vs `value`
pitfalls.consequence: Records may be erroneously filtered out or crashes may occur for edge cases like `value=None`.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure extraction stays compatible with the Record shapes used in tests (single value, multi values, and lenient records with None).

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying `include_target` behavior in _FilterProcessor without adjusting test expectations
pitfalls.consequence: Filters may start deleting target-zone records when configuration intends to only exclude source-side records.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep `process_target_zone` returning `existing` unchanged when `include_target` is False.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing root NS identification conditions
pitfalls.consequence: Either fails to remove/validate the intended changes, or blocks unrelated NS records.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the exact checks: zone filter uses `not record.name` while plan filter uses `record.name == ''`.

key_sections: _FilterProcessor: include_target semantics for source vs target
_TypeBaseFilter / _NameBaseFilter / _ValueBaseFilter: how matching lists and regex are handled
_NetworkValueBaseFilter: CIDR parsing and A/AAAA-only filtering
IgnoreRootNsFilter and ExcludeRootNsChanges: root NS handling at zone vs plan levels
ZoneNameFilter: additional name-vs-zone logic beyond truncated excerpt
skip_unless_needed: Docstrings that only provide example YAML (only relevant if adjusting processor configuration interface)

related_files.relationship: Unit tests that assert record/plan filtering outcomes for each filter class in this module.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavior change in filter matching/removal/include_target/root-NS identification will require updating these expectations.

related_files.relationship: Defines BaseProcessor interface used by these processors (process_source_zone/process_target_zone/process_plan).
related_files.reason_to_check: When editing processor classes here, confirm BaseProcessor expectations for signature/return values and logging.

related_files.relationship: ValidationError type raised by ExcludeRootNsChanges.
related_files.reason_to_check: Ensure raised ValidationError arguments match what tests and callers expect (fqdn, reasons, context).

insights.title: Value allow/reject filters crash when record value is None
insights.problem: Value-based filters assume record.value is present; when a record has an empty/None value, attempting to access .rdata_text raises AttributeError.
insights.root_cause: In _ValueBaseFilter processing, the code path for non-multi-value records unconditionally dereferences record.value and calls .rdata_text.
insights.solution: Wrap the record.value.rdata_text extraction in a try/except AttributeError and emit a warning log identifying the record fqdn; continue evaluation safely.
insights.lesson_learned: When filtering on record rdata/value, treat None/empty values as first-class inputs. Add coverage for records with value=None (lenient records) to prevent filter crashes.

insights.title: Add per-filter logger instances for better diagnostics
insights.problem: Warning logs from filter processors were less contextual than needed (e.g., which specific value filter instance produced the message).
insights.root_cause: The filter classes did not initialize instance-specific loggers using their filter name.
insights.solution: Initialize loggers in ValueAllowlistFilter and ValueRejectlistFilter constructors using getLogger(f'ValueAllowlistFilter[{name}]') / getLogger(f'ValueRejectlistFilter[{name}]').
insights.lesson_learned: Use instance-scoped loggers (keyed by filter name) to make troubleshooting in complex pipelines significantly easier.

insights.title: Avoid recreating ip list for each CIDR membership check
insights.problem: Within `_NetworkValueBaseFilter._process`, ipaddress conversion was performed repeatedly as part of the nested membership/product logic.
insights.root_cause: Inefficient generator expression that repeatedly called `ip_address(value)` while iterating over networks for each record.
insights.solution: Precomputed `ips = [ip_address(value) for value in record.values]` once per record, then used `product(ips, self.networks)` and checked `ip in network`.
insights.lesson_learned: When filtering with nested iteration (cartesian products), precompute expensive conversions once per outer loop to reduce CPU overhead.

insights.title: Refactor filter processors to share include_target logic
insights.problem: Many filter processors had duplicated target-zone filtering logic and lacked a consistent mechanism to optionally leave the target zone untouched.
insights.root_cause: Repeated `process_target_zone` implementations across filter processors; configuration feature (`include_target`) needed to be consistent.
insights.solution: Introduced `_FilterProcessor` with `include_target` flag; refactored shared filter base classes (e.g., `_TypeBaseFilter`, `_NameBaseFilter`, `ZoneNameFilter`) to inherit from it and funnel processing through `_process`.
insights.lesson_learned: For cross-cutting configuration like “include_target”, extract a shared base class rather than scattering conditional behavior across each filter.
insights.notes: This also required careful test updates to ensure default behavior remained unchanged while enabling the opt-out behavior.

insights.title: Move _NetworkValueBaseFilter definition after dependent classes
insights.problem: Module structure/ordering required reorganization, likely to satisfy readability or lint/import graph constraints.
insights.root_cause: The class was defined earlier but later needed to appear below its children (or vice versa) for maintainability.
insights.solution: Moved `_NetworkValueBaseFilter` definition down with its children in `octodns/processor/filter.py` without changing behavior.
insights.lesson_learned: When refactoring for maintainability, preserve behavior and add/keep tests that cover the affected logic to ensure the move is semantics-neutral.

octodns/record/base.py.short_description: Core DNS Record base with type registration, validation, copy/data, and shared mixins.
octodns/record/base.py.description:
This module defines the abstract `Record` base class and two mixins (`ValuesMixin`, `ValueMixin`) that implement shared logic for record types that hold multiple values or a single value. It is the central creation/validation path used by the rest of the record system: concrete record classes register themselves via `Record.register_type`, and `Record.new` uses that registry to select the proper concrete class, validate input, normalize names (including IDNA encoding), and return an instantiated record.

The design relies on a few important conventions that other parts of the codebase depend on. First, record names are normalized to an internal IDNA (punycode) form (`Record.__init__` encodes to `self.name`, while `decoded_name` preserves the human-readable form for logs/errors).
