pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the current behavior: only format `target` and construct a new `SrvValue` for templated outputs.

pitfalls.mistake: Relaxing `SrvValue.__init__` integer casting behavior
pitfalls.consequence: If priority/weight/port stop being cast to `int`, downstream equality, hashing, and record rendering (`rdata_text`) may become inconsistent.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect that `SrvValue.__init__()` always casts `priority`, `weight`, and `port` to `int`.

key_sections: SrvValue.parse_rdata_text: token split and unquoting behavior for SRV RDATA
SrvValue.validate: reason strings, numeric coercion checks, and delegated target validation
SrvValue.template: placeholder gating (`'{' in target`) and formatting only the target
SrvRecord.validate: SRV name regex enforcement before calling superclass validate
skip_unless_needed: SrvValue property setters/getters (straight-through dict access)
SrvValue.__hash__ (hashing strategy via repr) unless adjusting equality semantics

related_files.relationship: MX record implements analogous value parsing/validation/templating patterns (including target validation delegation and templating behavior).
related_files.reason_to_check: If adjusting shared validation/templating conventions or error-message formats, MX is the closest structural analog for consistency.

related_files.relationship: Provides `validate_target_fqdn()` and target value templating/IDNA behavior used by SRV.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any changes to FQDN validation rules, placeholder bypass logic, or trailing-dot/IDNA rules will directly affect SRV validation outcomes.

insights.title: Delegate SRV target validation to validate_target_fqdn() with correct key
insights.problem: SRV errors and templating bypass weren’t aligned with the standardized validation function.
insights.root_cause: SRV used inline FQDN validation instead of shared target validator.
insights.solution: Replaced inline FQDN/trailing-dot logic with `validate_target_fqdn(target, _type, 'target')`.
insights.lesson_learned: Keep record-type validation consistent by routing to a shared validator and ensuring field-key labeling is correct.

insights.title: `SrvValue.template` formats the target field
insights.problem: No templating support for SRV target placeholders.
insights.root_cause: Missing `template()` implementation for SRV values.
insights.solution: Add `template(params)` that formats `target` when it contains `{`, returning a new `SrvValue` instance.
insights.lesson_learned: Keep formatting behavior field-scoped (here `target`) to prevent accidental changes to numeric components.

octodns/record/chunked.py.short_description: Chunking/escaping helpers for TXT/SPF-like values and a wrapper value type.
octodns/record/chunked.py.description:
This module provides two internal building blocks used by “chunked” record types that need to split a long string into multiple quoted substrings (e.g., DNS TXT/SPF formatting constraints). `_ChunkedValuesMixin` implements the chunking logic: it escapes embedded double quotes, then splits the resulting string into segments no larger than `CHUNK_SIZE` (255 by default), while also avoiding splitting in a way that would break escape sequences (specifically, it checks for a trailing backslash at the chunk boundary). It then joins chunks using the DNS “adjacent string” style (`" "`), and wraps the final combined representation using the record’s configured `_value_type`.

`_ChunkedValue` is a string subclass that centralizes validation and parsing/formatting behaviors for the chunked string representation. It supports input that may already be wrapped in quotes by `process()`, performs semantic validation around semicolon escaping rules (single semicolon vs double-escaped semicolon), and enforces ASCII-only content by attempting an `encode('ascii')` during `validate()`. It also provides `parse_rdata_text()` to escape literal semicolons into `\;` when converting from provider RDATA text into the internal value form, and `template()` to apply Python `.format(**params)` only when templating braces exist in the string.

Within the broader system, this file is used by chunked record value implementations (e.g., TXT/SPF) that rely on mixins/wrappers to produce a provider-safe “rdata_text” representation while maintaining consistent validation. The provided tests indicate that correctness is sensitive to edge cases: non-string inputs passed to `parse_rdata_text`, required escaping of `;`, ASCII enforcement for TXT/SPF content, and exact behavior of chunk splitting around escape boundaries.

key_constructs.name: _ChunkedValuesMixin
key_constructs.purpose: Mixin implementing DNS-style chunking and quoting for long TXT/SPF-like values.
key_constructs.reasoning: The record layer can set `CHUNK_SIZE` (as tests do via a subclass) and supply `_value_type` so this mixin can return values in the exact wrapper/type expected by the record implementations.

key_constructs.name: chunked_value
key_constructs.purpose: Escape quotes, split into `CHUNK_SIZE` pieces, avoid breaking trailing backslashes, and join chunks.
key_constructs.reasoning: The method must produce a single value object of the configured `_value_type` containing the exact chunked/quoted representation that DNS/TXT providers expect.

key_constructs.name: chunked_values
key_constructs.purpose: Convert `self.values` into a list of chunked/wrapped values.
key_constructs.reasoning: Record implementations likely treat each input string in `self.values` independently, producing one chunked output per original value.

key_constructs.name: rr_values
key_constructs.purpose: Alias that exposes `chunked_values` as the record’s “resource record values.”
key_constructs.reasoning: Upstream code typically consumes `rr_values`; this keeps chunking logic wired into the standard record value pipeline.

key_constructs.name: _ChunkedValue
key_constructs.purpose: String wrapper that validates and processes chunked TXT/SPF strings and supports templating.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizing validation and transformation ensures consistent behavior regardless of which record/provider produces or consumes the value.

key_constructs.name: parse_rdata_text
key_constructs.purpose: Convert provider RDATA text into internal representation by escaping semicolons.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provider APIs often return semicolons unescaped; this normalizes input so later validation and output remain consistent.

key_constructs.name: validate
key_constructs.purpose: Return a list of reason strings describing semantic/encoding validation failures.
key_constructs.reasoning: Validation is used by the system to prevent producing invalid TXT/SPF content; it must match exact reason strings asserted in tests.

key_constructs.name: process
key_constructs.purpose: Normalize user/provider values by stripping outer quotes and removing the literal `" "` join artifact.
key_constructs.reasoning: This ensures the internal wrapper stores a clean unjoined string form while tolerating different incoming formats.

key_constructs.name: template
key_constructs.purpose: Apply `.format(**params)` when the string contains `{`, otherwise return itself unchanged.
key_constructs.reasoning: Some record value objects are expected to support templating uniformly; tests confirm chunked wrapper values must participate in that protocol.

key_behaviors: Escapes embedded double quotes before chunking
Splits values into substrings of at most `CHUNK_SIZE`, joined as adjacent quoted strings
Avoids chunk boundary splits that would leave a trailing backslash (escape sequence safety)
Escapes unescaped semicolons in provider RDATA text via `parse_rdata_text`
Validates semicolon escaping correctness via regex checks
Enforces ASCII-only content by attempting `encode('ascii')` during validation
Strips wrapper quotes and removes `" "` join artifacts in `process`
Applies templating only when `{` exists in the string

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the join format or quoting structure (e.g., not using the exact `" "` separator).
pitfalls.consequence: Round-tripping with providers and the chunk-boundary behavior expected by `process()` and tests will diverge, producing incorrect `rdata_text` content.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the exact string shapes asserted by tests (including outer quotes and the adjacent-string separator).

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying semicolon regex logic or the exact reason strings emitted by `validate()`.
pitfalls.consequence: Existing validation tests (and likely downstream consumers) will fail due to mismatched semantics or reason text drift.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat regexes and reason message strings as part of the tested contract.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or changing the `try/except AttributeError` in `parse_rdata_text`.
pitfalls.consequence: Non-string inputs (e.g., numbers) would raise errors when `.replace` isn’t available.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep `parse_rdata_text` tolerant to non-string values as the tests expect.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing chunk-boundary escape handling without accounting for escape sequences that span boundaries.
pitfalls.consequence: The emitted chunking may break `\;` or other escape sequences, producing invalid TXT/SPF text.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing boundary safety loop that adjusts chunk size when a backslash would be split.

_ChunkedValue.parse_rdata_text: semicolon escaping normalization for provider text
_ChunkedValue.validate: semantic + ASCII enforcement and reason strings
_ChunkedValue.process and template: normalization and templating protocol
skip_unless_needed: _unescaped_semicolon_re and _double_escaped_semicolon_re: only relevant if adjusting semicolon semantics

Covers validate() behaviors: missing, unescaped semicolon, double-escaped semicolon, non-ASCII.
Uses a smaller CHUNK_SIZE mixin to assert exact chunking output around escape boundaries.
Ensures template() returns same instance if no '{' and formats otherwise.

related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to chunk/join format, semicolon validation, ASCII validation, or templating short-circuit behavior must keep these assertions passing.

related_files.relationship: SPF record relies on the same chunked value behavior for `values[...].rdata_text` normalization.
related_files.reason_to_check: The test instantiates SpfRecord and checks rdata_text; verify that modifications in chunked helpers still match record expectations.

related_files.relationship: TXT record defines `_value_type` used by chunked mixin tests (TxtValue).
related_files.reason_to_check: Chunked helpers return `_value_type` objects; any behavioral change must remain compatible with TxtValue.

insights.title: Add `template()` to chunked record value wrapper
insights.problem: Templating protocol wasn’t present for chunked/string wrapper values.
insights.root_cause: Missing `template()` in `_ChunkedValue`.
insights.solution: Implement `template(params)` on `_ChunkedValue` to short-circuit if no `{` and otherwise apply `.format(**params)`.
insights.lesson_learned: Ensure wrapper/utility value classes implement the protocol too, not only the top-level record value classes.

insights.title: Reject non-ASCII characters in ChunkedValue (TXT/SPF)
insights.problem: Chunked values for TXT/SPF could contain non-ASCII characters, producing invalid DNS record content (or provider-dependent failures).
insights.root_cause: ChunkedValue.validate did not enforce an ASCII-only constraint for the value type(s) where it matters (TXT/SPF).
insights.solution: In _ChunkedValue.validate, attempt value.encode('ascii') and add a validation reason when UnicodeEncodeError occurs.
insights.lesson_learned: When DNS record formats have strict encoding requirements, enforce them at validation time and add regression tests for representative failing strings (e.g., SPF/TXT with Unicode punctuation/letters).

tests/test_octodns_record_chunked.py.short_description: Tests chunked TXT/SPF value parsing, validation, chunk splitting, and templating.
tests/test_octodns_record_chunked.py.description:
This test module exercises the chunking/escaping/value-wrapping functionality implemented in `octodns/record/chunked.py`. It verifies three major aspects: (1) `parse_rdata_text()` correctly handles non-string inputs (no-op) and escapes semicolons in string inputs, (2) `_ChunkedValue.validate()` produces the expected list of reason strings for semantic issues (missing values, unescaped semicolons, double-escaped semicolons) and for encoding constraints (rejecting non-ASCII characters), and (3) the chunking algorithm in `_ChunkedValuesMixin` splits strings into correctly quoted adjacent substrings without breaking escape sequences.

The splitting test intentionally sets `CHUNK_SIZE` to a small number (8) via a nested `SmallerChunkedMixin` to make boundary conditions manageable. It checks exact output strings including outer quotes and the adjacent-string separator (`" "`). It also verifies that templating behavior is implemented on the wrapper value: `template()` should return the same object if there are no braces, and should produce a new value when formatting occurs.

The module also includes a minimal integration-style check that uses `SpfRecord` to confirm that `a.values[0].rdata_text` reflects the normalized `_ChunkedValue` representation for semicolons/escaping expectations. This ties the helper code into record construction behavior and helps ensure compatibility with the rest of the record/value pipeline.

key_constructs.reasoning: Validates normalization behavior both at the helper level and when embedded in an actual record.

key_constructs.reasoning: Defines the contract for validation messages and the exact expected chunking output.

key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures provider-text parsing rules and record integration remain consistent.

key_constructs.purpose: Asserts validation failures and expected reason strings for multiple inputs.
key_constructs.reasoning: Reason-string equality makes this test a strict contract for validate() output.

key_constructs.name: test_splitting
key_constructs.purpose: Asserts exact chunk splitting around size boundaries and escape sequences.
key_constructs.reasoning: Exact string comparisons ensure the chunk algorithm’s escape-boundary safety is preserved.

key_constructs.name: test_template
key_constructs.purpose: Asserts wrapper templating short-circuit and formatting behavior.
key_constructs.reasoning: Confirms the wrapper participates in the templating protocol expected by higher-level record logic.

Checks semicolon escaping contract for provider RDATA text
Checks validate returns exact reason lists for specific invalid cases

pitfalls.consequence: Exact string assertions (quotes, separators, escaping) will fail, indicating the system contract was broken.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat these tests as defining the precise text-format contract.

pitfalls.consequence: Tests use equality on the returned list contents; mismatches will fail even if semantics are equivalent.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve exact text of reason messages if behavior is maintained.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming chunk boundary behavior without covering escape spanning boundaries.
pitfalls.consequence: Breaking escape splitting may produce invalid DNS text; the test cases explicitly cover these scenarios.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the boundary scenarios (escape at boundary, spanning boundary, multiple escapes) in mind when editing.

test_template: instance identity expectations for no-op templating

Validation reason strings, including unicode/non-ASCII acceptance rejection.
Exact chunked_values expected for numerous escape boundary scenarios.
Template behavior: same-instance short-circuit and new formatted instance.

related_files.relationship: Defines the functions and classes under test: _ChunkedValuesMixin and _ChunkedValue.

insights.title: Coverage for ChunkedValue ASCII validation including TXT/SPF examples
insights.problem: ASCII validation needed regression coverage for both general validation structure (missing, unescaped semicolon) and the new non-ASCII rule.
insights.root_cause: Without tests, non-ASCII acceptance could remain unnoticed until a provider fails or an external system rejects the output.
insights.solution: Add TestChunkedValue.test_validate covering valid strings, missing value(s), unescaped semicolon, and specific unicode-containing SPF/TXT strings to ensure correct validation reasons.
insights.lesson_learned: For validation logic, include both semantic-invalid and encoding-invalid inputs with expected reason strings to avoid “reason drift” later.

octodns/processor/ownership.py.short_description: Processor that injects TXT ownership records and filters plan changes to those owned.
octodns/processor/ownership.py.description:
This module defines `OwnershipProcessor`, a BaseProcessor used to make synthetic “ownership” TXT records so octoDNS can safely modify or delete only the records it has been configured to manage. It participates in the processor pipeline in two phases: `process_source_zone` augments the desired zone with ownership TXT records for each desired record (excluding ownership TXT records themselves), and `process_plan` filters the computed plan’s change list to only apply changes for records it considers owned or special cases.

Ownership TXT records are named using the pattern `_{owner_prefix}.{record_type}[.{wildcard_name}]` and contain a single configured TXT value (default `*octodns*`) with a configured TTL (default `60`). Ownership detection in `_is_ownership` is strict: type must be `TXT`, the TXT name must start with `txt_name` (default `_owner`), and the record’s `values` must exactly match the list stored in `_txt_values`.

In `process_plan`, the processor derives an `owned` index by scanning both `plan.existing.records` and `plan.desired.records` for any ownership TXT records. It then walks `plan.changes` and keeps changes when either (a) the change record itself is an ownership TXT, or (b) the record’s `name` matches an owned name for its type, or (c) the record is the special `octodns-meta` record. If filtering removes all changes it returns `None` (indicating no action), otherwise it returns either the original `plan` or a new `Plan` object containing only the filtered changes.

This file also contains an explicit guard against duplicate ownership TXT generation via the `should_replace` flag: ownership TXT records are added to the desired zone with `replace=self.should_replace`. This behavior is important in multi-pass scenarios (notably alias zones), where the processor may see the same synthetic ownership target more than once.

key_constructs.name: OwnershipProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Injects ownership TXT records into desired zones and filters plan changes to owned records.
key_constructs.reasoning: The processor centralizes the synthetic ownership naming/value/TTL rules and enforces safe ownership-based filtering so other parts of octoDNS don’t accidentally delete/modify unmanaged records.

key_constructs.name: process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Creates ownership TXT records for desired records that are not already ownership TXT records.
key_constructs.reasoning: It’s the mechanism by which the desired state explicitly includes ownership markers, enabling later plan filtering.

key_constructs.name: process_plan
key_constructs.purpose: Filters plan changes to only those related to owned records and special handling.
key_constructs.reasoning: It prevents apply-time actions for unmanaged records while still allowing ownership TXT management and `octodns-meta`.

key_constructs.name: _is_ownership
key_constructs.purpose: Determines whether a record is an ownership TXT record matching configured prefix and exact configured values.
key_constructs.reasoning: Strict matching makes ownership markers unambiguous and avoids accidentally treating user TXT records as ownership markers.

key_behaviors: creates ownership TXT records in the desired zone for each managed record
skips generating ownership TXT for existing ownership TXT records
supports alias/multi-pass scenarios via replace semantics when adding TXT ownership records
identifies ownership from both desired and existing records during plan processing
keeps plan changes only for owned records, ownership TXT records, and the 'octodns-meta' special record
returns None when all planned changes are filtered out

pitfalls.mistake: Treating any TXT record as ownership without matching both name prefix and configured TXT values
pitfalls.consequence: User TXT records could be incorrectly considered owned, causing unintended modifications/deletions.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve strict `_is_ownership` semantics (type, name prefix, and exact `values` match).

pitfalls.mistake: Removing the skip for ownership TXT records inside `process_source_zone`
pitfalls.consequence: Ownership TXT records could recursively generate more ownership TXT records, or trigger duplicates in alias zones.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the conditional `if self._is_ownership(record): continue` behavior.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the ownership TXT naming scheme without updating plan ownership parsing
pitfalls.consequence: `process_plan`’s parsing of ownership TXT names would no longer map to the correct owned record names/types, breaking filtering logic.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep `process_source_zone` record naming and `process_plan` ownership TXT name parsing consistent.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering how `_wildcard` is represented in ownership TXT names
pitfalls.consequence: Wildcard record ownership attribution would mismatch, leading to either over-filtering (missed actions) or under-filtering (incorrect actions).
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the conversion `record.name.replace('*', '_wildcard')` and the reverse `name.replace('_wildcard', '*')` in `process_plan`.

pitfalls.mistake: Not preserving `octodns-meta` special-case behavior
pitfalls.consequence: Changes to `octodns-meta` could be suppressed, potentially breaking octoDNS metadata handling.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the explicit `record.name != 'octodns-meta'` guard in the filter logic.

key_sections: OwnershipProcessor.process_source_zone: how ownership TXT names are constructed and added to desired
OwnershipProcessor._is_ownership: strict criteria for recognizing ownership TXT records
OwnershipProcessor.process_plan: how it builds the owned index and filters plan changes
skip_unless_needed: Constructor parameter handling other than txt_name/txt_value/txt_ttl/should_replace

Asserts default ownership TXT TTL=60, and then sets ownership.txt_ttl=300 and revalidates.
Exercises process_plan: verifies noop when creating everything, leaves unmanaged extra records alone, and allows planned deletes when ownership TXT exists.
Ensures process_plan returns Falsey/None when filtering removes the only change in the plan.

related_files.relationship: Unit tests specify expected ownership TXT naming, TTL application, plan filtering behavior, and duplicate-handling via should_replace.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any edits to naming/value parsing, skip conditions, or filtering rules will likely require matching updates to these assertions.

related_files.relationship: Defines BaseProcessor contract (method names/signatures) that OwnershipProcessor implements.
related_files.reason_to_check: If processor lifecycle behavior or signature expectations change, OwnershipProcessor’s methods must remain compatible.

insights.title: OwnershipProcessor supports replacing ownership records to handle alias zones
insights.problem: When zones are processed multiple times (e.g., original zone + alias zone), OwnershipProcessor could attempt to add duplicate ownership TXT records and fail.
insights.root_cause: OwnershipProcessor lacked control over whether to replace existing ownership records; alias processing likely results in repeated generation for the same synthetic ownership record.
insights.solution: Commit 8c22040 adds should_replace to OwnershipProcessor and passes `replace=self.should_replace` to `desired.add_record(...)` for ownership TXT records.
insights.lesson_learned: Synthetic records generated by processors may be re-applied in multi-pass scenarios (aliases, copy-on-write zones, lifecycle phases). Provide replace semantics (or idempotency) explicitly.

insights.title: OwnershipProcessor allows configuring TXT TTL
insights.problem: OwnershipProcessor always used a fixed TTL (default 60) for the ownership TXT record, preventing customization for some environments.
insights.root_cause: OwnershipProcessor constructor had hardcoded ttl when building the desired TXT record.
insights.solution: Add txt_ttl parameter to OwnershipProcessor.__init__ (default 60) and use it when constructing the ownership TXT record.
insights.lesson_learned: Expose TTL-like operational parameters via processor configuration and cover both default and overridden values in tests to prevent regressions.

tests/test_octodns_processor_ownership.p.short_description: Unit tests verifying OwnershipProcessor ownership TXT generation and plan filtering logic.
tests/test_octodns_processor_ownership.p.description:
This test module defines a `TestOwnershipProcessor` suite that validates the observable behavior of `OwnershipProcessor` against a controlled in-memory DNS zone model. It constructs a shared `Zone` instance (`zone = Zone('unit.tests.', [])`) and multiple `Record` instances of different types, including a wildcard A record (record name `'*'`) and a TXT record. Those records are added into the zone prior to test execution.

The tests focus on four main areas: (1) `process_source_zone` generates the expected ownership TXT records (by name) for each managed record and correctly sets the ownership TXT value/TTL; (2) `process_plan` filters plan changes—ensuring that unmanaged records without ownership TXT markers are left alone, while records with ownership markers allow deletions to proceed; (3) when filtering removes all changes, `process_plan` returns a falsey result (the test uses `assertFalse(plan)` semantics); and (4) `should_replace` controls duplicate-handling when `process_source_zone` is called multiple times on zones that already contain ownership TXT records (duplicate case raises `DuplicateRecordException`, replacement case succeeds).

These tests implicitly encode the naming and parsing contract between `process_source_zone` and `process_plan`, including the wildcard naming translation (`'*'` to `'_wildcard'` in the ownership TXT name) and the special `octodns-meta` exemption behavior (covered indirectly through the filtering conditions). Any change to ownership name structure, value comparison, or filtering logic is likely to break one or more assertions here.

key_constructs.name: TestOwnershipProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Groups unit tests for ownership TXT injection and plan change filtering.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides coverage for both state augmentation (source zone processing) and enforcement (plan filtering).

key_constructs.purpose: Asserts generated ownership TXT names and correct value/TTL, and validates `_is_ownership` recognition.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures the sentinel mechanism matches exactly what `process_plan` later relies on.

key_constructs.name: test_process_plan
key_constructs.purpose: Validates plan filtering for owned vs unmanaged records and verifies ownership TXT deletions are permitted.
key_constructs.reasoning: Confirms the core safety mechanism: only apply changes for owned items.

key_constructs.name: test_remove_last_change
key_constructs.purpose: Ensures that if filtering eliminates the only change, the processor yields a falsey/no-op result.
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents generating plans that contain no actionable changes.

key_constructs.name: test_should_replace
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies duplicate ownership TXT generation fails by default and succeeds when `should_replace=True`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encodes historical behavior and multi-pass/alias-zone expectations.

key_behaviors: verifies ownership TXT record names are generated for each managed record
checks ownership TXT records are recognized by `_is_ownership`
validates ownership TXT TTL default and override behavior
ensures plan changes for unmanaged records are filtered out
ensures planned deletes of owned records (including ownership TXT deletes) are allowed
ensures plan is dropped when all changes are filtered out
ensures duplicate creation errors are thrown unless should_replace is enabled

pitfalls.consequence: Tests may pass for generation but fail for filtering, or vice versa, because the naming/value mapping is shared across both phases.
pitfalls.prevention: Run the full test module and keep generator/parsing behavior consistent.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing ownership TXT naming output without updating expected sorted name list
pitfalls.consequence: The `test_process_source_zone` assertions on exact record names will fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat the expected list as the canonical encoding for the sentinel naming scheme.

related_files.reason_to_check: Any modifications to OwnershipProcessor’s sentinel name parsing/generation or filtering rules will require aligning these tests.

insights.title: Add tests for OwnershipProcessor default and custom TXT TTL
insights.problem: No test explicitly verified the TTL applied to ownership TXT records or that the new configuration would take effect.
insights.root_cause: Coverage focused on ownership detection rather than TTL configurability.
insights.solution: Assert the default ownership TXT record ttl is 60, then set ownership.txt_ttl=300 and assert the processed ownership record ttl matches.

octodns/cmds/args.py.short_description: Defines CLI argument parsing and sets up logging (stderr/stdout + optional syslog).
octodns/cmds/args.py.description:
This module provides a specialized `ArgumentParser` subclass used by octoDNS CLI commands to standardize common command-line options and logging behavior. Its primary responsibility is to extend base `argparse.ArgumentParser` with version/help/logging flags, parse arguments, and then immediately configure Python logging accordingly. This ensures every CLI command that uses this parser gets consistent log formatting and log-level semantics (including debug/quiet behavior) without duplicating logging code across commands.

The main workflow is `parse_args()`, which adds standard logging-related flags (`--log-stream-stdout`, `--log-syslog`, `--syslog-device`, `--syslog-facility`, `--debug`, `--quiet`, `--logging-config`) plus `--version`. After `super().parse_args()` returns `args`, `_setup_logging(args, default_log_level)` is called. `_setup_logging` has two modes: if `args.logging_config` is provided, it loads a YAML logging configuration using `octodns.yaml.safe_load` and applies it via `logging.config.dictConfig`, then returns early (skipping all default formatter/handler setup). Otherwise, it constructs a `StreamHandler` writing to `stdout` or `stderr` based on `--log-stream-stdout`, optionally adds a `SysLogHandler` when `--log-syslog` is enabled, sets the root logger level to `default_log_level` (or overrides it for `--debug` and `--quiet`), and adjusts the `Plan` logger level so plans still appear when running in quiet mode.

This file also contains a formatter alignment decision: the default log line formatter uses a fixed-width field sized to the largest log level name being targeted (the comment references “warning”). That indicates prior attention to log readability/format stability. Any changes to log level handling or formatter strings need to respect this alignment intent so CLI output remains stable and comparable to existing expectations (including any golden-output tests, if present elsewhere in the repo).

key_constructs.name: ArgumentParser
key_constructs.purpose: Subclass of argparse parser that adds common octoDNS CLI flags and configures logging after parsing.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes CLI option definitions and logging setup so all CLI commands share consistent behavior and formatter logic.

key_constructs.name: parse_args
key_constructs.purpose: Adds version and logging-related CLI arguments, parses them, and calls `_setup_logging`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures logging is configured immediately based on parsed flags before the rest of the CLI runs.

key_constructs.name: _setup_logging
key_constructs.purpose: Applies either a user-provided logging YAML config or the built-in stream/syslog logging configuration, then sets logger levels.
key_constructs.reasoning: Contains the branching behavior between `--logging-config` and default handler/formatter setup, including quiet/debug semantics for the root and Plan loggers.

key_behaviors: adds a `--version` flag that prints the octoDNS version
adds flags to route log output to stdout vs stderr
optionally adds syslog logging when `--log-syslog` is provided
loads and applies a YAML logging configuration when `--logging-config` is set
sets root logger level to `default_log_level`, overridden by `--debug` (DEBUG) and `--quiet` (WARNING)
when `--quiet` is set, sets the `Plan` logger level to INFO so plan output is still produced

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or bypassing the early return when `args.logging_config` is provided.
pitfalls.consequence: Built-in handlers/formatters may be added in addition to (or conflicting with) the dictConfig-installed configuration, leading to duplicate logs or unexpected formatting.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the conditional branch that returns immediately after `dictConfig(config)`.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing logger level behavior without accounting for the special-case `getLogger('Plan').setLevel(INFO)` in quiet mode.
pitfalls.consequence: In quiet mode, plan output may disappear, changing user-facing CLI behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the intent that quiet suppresses most logs but still allows plan logging to be emitted.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering formatter alignment fields that assume specific log level string widths.
pitfalls.consequence: Log line alignment/readability changes (and possibly snapshot/golden tests elsewhere) even if functionality remains correct.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat the formatter string and its fixed-width comment as part of expected output stability.

pitfalls.mistake: Switching from `stdout`/`stderr` selection to a different stream or incorrectly handling `--log-stream-stdout`.
pitfalls.consequence: Users expecting stream separation (e.g., piping output while still seeing logs) will get mixed or redirected output.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the mapping: `--log-stream-stdout` -> stdout else stderr.

key_sections: parse_args: argument definitions for version and logging flags; calls _setup_logging
_setup_logging: logging-config YAML path vs default stream/syslog setup; sets root level and Plan logger level
skip_unless_needed: imports at top (unless you need to trace logging utilities or safe_load behavior)

related_files.relationship: Provides `safe_load` used to parse the `--logging-config` YAML logging configuration.
related_files.reason_to_check: If logging YAML behavior changes, this file’s config loading path is affected.

related_files.relationship: CLI command entry points likely instantiate this `ArgumentParser` and depend on `parse_args()` to configure logging.
related_files.reason_to_check: To understand how `default_log_level` is chosen and how the parsed args are used after logging is configured.

related_files.relationship: This module explicitly references `getLogger('Plan')` to control plan visibility under `--quiet`.
related_files.reason_to_check: To confirm what logger name(s) are actually used for plan output and how that affects quiet mode.

insights.title: Fix log level formatting to align WARNING output
insights.problem: Logging format alignment differed due to deprecated/renamed log levels (WARN vs WARNING), making log lines visually inconsistent.
insights.root_cause: The formatter used a field width sized for the previous log level string length assumptions.
insights.solution: Updated the formatter format string to align WARNING (field width adjusted; comment explains the chosen width).
insights.lesson_learned: When migrating log levels (e.g., WARN->WARNING), also validate formatter alignment and update both code + tests/expected output where applicable.

octodns/processor/restrict.py.short_description: Processor that restricts record TTL values within min/max or an allowed set.
octodns/processor/restrict.py.description:
This module defines a TTL restriction processor used in octoDNS' processor pipeline. Its role is to validate (and thereby block planning/sync behavior) when source records have TTL values outside configured constraints. The processor runs at the zone level via BaseProcessor’s hook `process_source_zone`, iterating through `zone.records` and validating each record’s TTL unless the record is marked lenient.

Configuration behavior is centered on three mutually-related constraints: `min_ttl`, `max_ttl`, and `allowed_ttls`. When `allowed_ttls` is explicitly configured, the processor enforces membership in that set and ignores `min_ttl`/`max_ttl` (as described in the class docstring and implemented by checking `self.allowed_ttls` first). When `allowed_ttls` is not configured, the processor enforces numeric bounds: TTL must be >= `min_ttl` and <= `max_ttl`. Violations raise `RestrictionException`, a subclass of `ProcessorException`, which higher layers in the system will treat as a processor failure.

Leniency is enforced per-record: the processor skips validation for any record where `record.lenient` is true. This file intentionally does not consult higher-level leniency flags; only the record-level lenient accessor is used. That means if zone-level or other metadata marks leniency, it must ultimately translate into `record.lenient` for the skip to occur. The exception messages include record `fqdn`, the offending TTL, and the constraint values to aid diagnostics in CLI/plan output.

key_constructs.name: RestrictionException
key_constructs.purpose: Signals a TTL restriction violation during processor execution.
key_constructs.reasoning: It subclasses `ProcessorException` so the rest of octoDNS can handle failures consistently (e.g., abort processing/emit plan errors) while preserving a specific exception type for this processor.

key_constructs.name: TtlRestrictionFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Validates each record TTL against min/max or allowed values within a zone.
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements the processor pipeline contract by overriding `process_source_zone` and raising `RestrictionException` when a record violates configured TTL policies. The ordering of checks (allowed set first, then min/max) encodes the “allowed_ttls overrides min/max” semantics.

key_constructs.name: process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Performs TTL validation for all records in a source zone.
key_constructs.reasoning: Iterates `zone.records`, conditionally skips records marked `lenient`, applies `allowed_ttls` membership validation when configured, otherwise checks `min_ttl` and `max_ttl`, and returns the (unchanged) zone on success.

key_behaviors: Validates record TTLs during `process_source_zone` by iterating `zone.records`
Skips TTL validation for records where `record.lenient` is true
If `allowed_ttls` is configured, enforces TTL is in the allowed set and ignores min/max
If `allowed_ttls` is not configured, enforces TTL is within [min_ttl, max_ttl]
Raises `RestrictionException` with diagnostic details when a record violates the policy
Returns the input `zone` unchanged after successful validation

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming that lenient flags at a higher level (zone/config-level) will automatically skip validation.
pitfalls.consequence: Validation may still fail because this processor only checks `record.lenient` during iteration.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure that whatever config/metadata marks leniency results in `Record.lenient` being true for the specific record objects processed here.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming `min_ttl`/`max_ttl` still apply when `allowed_ttls` is configured.
pitfalls.consequence: Records can pass or fail based solely on set membership rather than numeric bounds, leading to unexpected enforcement behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: When configuring, treat `allowed_ttls` as an override: confirm records comply with the allowed set rather than min/max.

pitfalls.mistake: Raising a different exception type than `RestrictionException` (or not subclassing `ProcessorException`) in edits.
pitfalls.consequence: The rest of octoDNS may not handle the failure uniformly, possibly causing inconsistent error reporting or processor behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the exception type relationship (`RestrictionException` -> `ProcessorException`) when changing validation/error paths.

key_sections: TtlRestrictionFilter docstring: documents min/max defaults, allowed_ttls override semantics, and lenient behavior
TtlRestrictionFilter.__init__: initializes min/max/allowed_ttls, including the allowed set conversion and None handling
process_source_zone: the exact validation order and the specific exception messages
skip_unless_needed: RestrictionException class body (it is a pass-through subclass)

related_files.relationship: Defines the processor interface/hook methods (including `process_source_zone`) and the base exception type `ProcessorException` this module extends.
related_files.reason_to_check: If processor hook contracts or exception handling change, this processor’s integration may need to match that behavior.

related_files.relationship: Provides the `Record` object interface, including the `lenient` accessor used here and `ttl`/`fqdn` attributes referenced in error messages.
related_files.reason_to_check: Edits to record fields/accessors (e.g., `lenient`, `ttl`, `fqdn`) can affect how this processor performs checks.

insights.title: Use Record.lenient instead of raw _octodns lookup
insights.problem: Code directly accessed `record._octodns.get('lenient')` instead of using a stable interface.
insights.root_cause: Missing standardized `Record.lenient` accessor; tight coupling to `_octodns` internal dict.
insights.solution: Replaced `if record._octodns.get('lenient')` with `if record.lenient`.
insights.lesson_learned: Prefer public accessors over internal metadata dicts to avoid regressions when `_octodns` structure changes.

octodns/processor/trailing_dots.py.short_description: Processor that appends missing trailing dots to specific DNS record fields.
octodns/processor/trailing_dots.py.description:
This module defines `EnsureTrailingDots`, a `BaseProcessor` that normalizes certain DNS record value fields by ensuring they end with a trailing dot. It is designed to run during source-zone processing, mutating the desired record set by replacing records whose relevant fields are missing the terminal period. The processor covers multiple RR types: `ALIAS`, `CNAME`, `DNAME` (single `.value` string-like field), `NS` and `PTR` (list-like `.values` fields), and `MX` and `SRV` (structured values with sub-fields like `exchange`/`target`).

A key design constraint is type preservation of record value objects. The helper `_ensure_trailing_dots` appends a dot while explicitly constructing the same concrete value class (via `val.__class__(...)`) to avoid coercing RR-specific value types back into plain strings. The main `process_source_zone` method uses this same strategy: it creates a record copy, then either replaces `new.value` with an instance of the original value’s class, or (for list values) builds a new `new.values` list using the class of the first element (`klass = new.values[0].__class__`). This is important because downstream code may expect methods/properties provided by those specialized RR value classes.

The processor is leniency-aware: `process_source_zone(..., lenient=False)` combines its processor-level `self.lenient` setting with the call-site `lenient` using `lenient = self.lenient or lenient`. When it adds modified records back into the zone, it passes through `lenient` to `desired.add_record(..., replace=True, lenient=lenient)`. That leniency matters in scenarios like managed sub-zones where record additions can fail without lenient behavior.

key_constructs.name: _no_trailing_dot
key_constructs.purpose: Checks whether any value in a record field lacks a trailing dot.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to decide whether `MX`/`SRV`/list-based RR types actually need normalization; its return value drives whether record replacement occurs.

key_constructs.name: _ensure_trailing_dots
key_constructs.purpose: Returns a copy of a record with a single structured field forced to have trailing dots.
key_constructs.reasoning: Appends `.` to values in a field while preserving the concrete value type by using `val.__class__(f'{val}.')`.

key_constructs.name: EnsureTrailingDots
key_constructs.purpose: Processor that normalizes record fields to always include trailing dots.
key_constructs.reasoning: Central entry point; integrates with `BaseProcessor` lifecycle and updates the desired record set.

key_constructs.name: process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Iterates desired records and replaces any record whose targeted fields are missing trailing dots.
key_constructs.reasoning: The function encapsulates per-RR-type logic and leniency behavior; it is what the tests validate.

key_behaviors: Replaces records in the desired set when specific fields are missing trailing dots
Preserves RR value concrete classes when appending trailing dots
Handles RR types: ALIAS/CNAME/DNAME (record.value), NS/PTR (record.values), MX/SRV (structured sub-fields)
Combines processor-level lenient setting with call-site lenient

pitfalls.mistake: Coercing value fields to plain strings when appending trailing dots (e.g., using f'{val}.' directly without preserving class).
pitfalls.consequence: Returned RR value objects may no longer be instances of expected specialized value classes, breaking downstream code and tests that assert type preservation.
pitfalls.prevention: When modifying fields, continue using `val.__class__(...)` (or equivalent class-carrying logic for list values) as in the existing helpers.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the per-RR-type field being normalized (e.g., using `.target` when the record expects `.exchange` for MX, or modifying the wrong attribute for list-based types).
pitfalls.consequence: Normalization will be incomplete or incorrect, causing plan differences or leaving malformed values unchanged.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing mapping of RR type to field: ALIAS/CNAME/DNAME -> `record.value`; NS/PTR -> `record.values`; MX -> `exchange`; SRV -> `target`.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing leniency propagation semantics (e.g., ignoring `self.lenient` or call-site `lenient`).
pitfalls.consequence: Record replacement/addition may raise sub-zone-related exceptions instead of operating successfully in managed sub-zones.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the `lenient = self.lenient or lenient` behavior and pass `lenient` into `desired.add_record(..., lenient=lenient)`.

key_sections: ALIAS/CNAME/DNAME branch: appends dot to `record.value` while preserving value class
NS/PTR branch: appends dot to each element of `record.values` using the class from the first value
MX/SRV branches: uses `_no_trailing_dot` and `_ensure_trailing_dots` for structured values (exchange/target)
Leniency: `lenient = self.lenient or lenient` and how it's passed to `desired.add_record`
skip_unless_needed: Helpers `_no_trailing_dot` and `_ensure_trailing_dots` are small but central to the MX/SRV logic

relevant_snippets.description: CNAME/ALIAS/DNAME tests assert both string content and instance types of `.value`.
MX test uses `_no_trailing_dot` and `_ensure_trailing_dots` directly; asserts list of exchanges gets dots appended.
NS/PTR/SRV tests assert `.values` elements are instances of `NsValue`/`PtrValue` and targets/exchanges get normalized.
Leniency tests ensure processor-level lenient and call-level lenient both allow record replacement under managed sub-zones.

related_files.relationship: Unit tests validate all RR-type branches plus type-preservation and leniency behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: If editing normalization logic, tests will indicate whether the correct fields were modified and whether value classes are preserved.

insights.title: EnsureTrailingDots: preserve value types when appending trailing dots
insights.problem: EnsureTrailingDots previously coerced record value types back to plain strings, breaking downstream code that expected specialized RR value classes (e.g., `AliasValue`, `CnameValue`) and caused failures when RR methods were invoked.
insights.root cause: The processor set properties using string concatenation (`f'{val}.'`) without preserving the existing value class; while string subclasses existed, type coercion was not always safe across record/value implementations.
insights.solution: Changed the helper to set values using `val.__class__(f'{val}.')` and similarly for list values in `NS`/`PTR` cases by capturing the class from the original values (`klass = new.values[0].__class__`).
insights.lesson_learned: If processors mutate value-like objects, preserve their concrete types—especially when later code relies on methods/properties on those specific value classes.

tests/test_octodns_processor_trailing_do.short_description: Unit tests for EnsureTrailingDots normalization and value-type preservation.
tests/test_octodns_processor_trailing_do.description:
This test module verifies the behavior of `octodns.processor.trailing_dots.EnsureTrailingDots` and its helper functions `_ensure_trailing_dots` and `_no_trailing_dot`. It constructs `Zone` instances with in-memory `Record` objects using `Record.new`, then adds those records to the zone and runs the processor via `process_source_zone`. The tests focus on two dimensions: (1) content normalization (appending a trailing dot when missing) and (2) preservation of the concrete RR value classes (`CnameValue`, `AliasValue`, `DnameValue`, `NsValue`, `PtrValue`).

For MX and SRV, tests explicitly validate both the predicate/helper logic and the final processor output. The MX test calls `_no_trailing_dot(record, 'exchange')` to assert the predicate correctly detects missing dots, and calls `_ensure_trailing_dots(record, 'exchange')` to assert the fix produces the expected normalized exchanges. For `NS`/`PTR`, tests also confirm returned list elements maintain the expected value object types. Additionally, two leniency tests cover behavior under managed sub-zones: a CNAME record that would fail without leniency can be processed successfully when the processor call or configuration sets `lenient=True`.

key_constructs.name: EnsureTrailingDotsTest
key_constructs.purpose: Test suite for EnsureTrailingDots processor and related helper functions.
key_constructs.reasoning: Groups tests by RR type and asserts both normalized strings and typed value objects.

key_behaviors: Asserts processor appends missing trailing dots for CNAME/ALIAS/DNAME record `.value`
Asserts processor appends missing trailing dots for NS/PTR record `.values` while preserving value types
Asserts helper predicate and fixer behave correctly for MX `exchange` and SRV `target`
Asserts leniency handling allows processing records under managed sub-zones

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying tests to only compare string equality without asserting instance types for `.value`/`.values` elements.
pitfalls.consequence: A regression that breaks value-type preservation could go unnoticed and still pass content-only assertions.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the explicit `assertIsInstance` checks for the specialized value classes.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing how lenient is invoked in tests without maintaining the separation between processor-level and call-level leniency.
pitfalls.consequence: You may reduce coverage of how `lenient = self.lenient or lenient` influences record addition under sub-zones.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve `test_lenient_with_sub_zones` and `test_lenient_from_config` semantics (call-level vs processor-level).

key_sections: test_cname/test_alias/test_dname: `.value` normalization + type preservation
test_mx: predicate `_no_trailing_dot` + fixer `_ensure_trailing_dots` + final processor output
test_ns/test_ptr: `.values` list normalization + value type assertions
test_srv: predicate/fixer coverage for structured `target` plus processor output
test_lenient_with_sub_zones/test_lenient_from_config: leniency behavior under managed sub-zones
skip_unless_needed: The local `_find` helper is only for looking up records by name after processing

related_files.relationship: Defines the processor and helper functions that these tests exercise directly.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any semantic change in field selection, dot-appending rules, type preservation, or leniency propagation will be reflected here.

insights.title: Add tests ensuring EnsureTrailingDots preserves RR value subclass types
insights.problem: Existing tests may have only checked string content, not that value objects kept their specialized types.
insights.root cause: Type regressions only surface when downstream methods are called on the value objects.
insights.solution: Expanded tests to assert the returned `.value` / `.values[]` are instances of the correct classes (e.g., `AliasValue`, `CnameValue`, `DnameValue`, `NsValue`, `PtrValue`). Also removed prior test hacks that mutably changed `_type` and instead created records with the proper types.
insights.lesson_learned: When a bug is about type preservation, update tests to assert types (not just string equality). Prefer constructing real typed records over mutating internals for test coverage.

octodns/record/__init__.py.short_description: Exports all DNS record and value classes as the octodns.record public API.
octodns/record/__init__.py.description:
This module acts as the package-level façade for the `octodns.record` namespace. It imports record classes (e.g., `ARecord`, `AaaaRecord`, `AliasRecord`, etc.) and their associated value types (e.g., `Ipv4Value`, `Ipv6Value`, `AliasValue`, etc.) from submodules and re-exports them.

The long trailing section (lines consisting only of names like `ARecord`, `Update`, etc.) is used to silence lint/type-checker warnings about imported-but-unused symbols. This is a common pattern to ensure that these re-exports are treated as intentionally public, even though the file itself does not reference the imported names beyond that suppression block.

In the wider system, other components (planner/sync tooling, provider/manager logic, or user-facing configuration parsing) typically need access to record constructors/validation types without needing to know which specific submodule implements a given record. By centralizing exports here, the rest of the codebase can import from `octodns.record` consistently, and tooling that enumerates record classes can rely on this package as the stable import surface.

key_constructs.name: ARecord
key_constructs.purpose: Re-export of the A (IPv4) DNS record class from `octodns.record.a`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Callers depend on a stable import path (`octodns.record.ARecord`) rather than submodule paths.

key_constructs.name: AaaaRecord
key_constructs.purpose: Re-export of the AAAA (IPv6) DNS record class from `octodns.record.aaaa`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Maintains consistent package-level API for IPv6 record construction/validation.

key_constructs.name: AliasRecord
key_constructs.purpose: Re-export of the Alias record class from `octodns.record.alias`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures alias-related behavior is accessible via the package API.

key_constructs.name: Record
key_constructs.purpose: Re-export of the base `Record` class from `octodns.record.base`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Other system layers often target the common base type when working with heterogeneous record sets.

key_constructs.name: Change
key_constructs.purpose: Re-export of the `Change` class from `octodns.record.change`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Planning/sync likely represents diffs using these change types and imports them from the package.

key_constructs.name: Create
key_constructs.purpose: Re-export of the `Create` change subclass from `octodns.record.change`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to express desired mutations in generated plans and for sync execution logic.

key_constructs.name: Delete
key_constructs.purpose: Re-export of the `Delete` change subclass from `octodns.record.change`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to represent removal operations in plan output and application.

key_constructs.name: Update
key_constructs.purpose: Re-export of the `Update` change subclass from `octodns.record.change`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to represent modifications between current and desired record states.

key_constructs.name: ValidationError
key_constructs.purpose: Re-export of validation-related exception type from `octodns.record.exception`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Upstream configuration parsing/validation flows can catch this consistently.

handles_entities: DNS record types (A, AAAA, CNAME, etc.)
key_behaviors: Re-exports record classes and value types from submodules under `octodns.record.*`
Ensures static analyzers treat imported symbols as intentionally public
Provides a stable import surface for plan/sync/config parsing code to use

pitfalls.mistake: Changing or removing an import name without understanding external imports from `octodns.record`
pitfalls.consequence: Breaks downstream code that imports record/value/change classes via the package namespace, causing runtime ImportError or attribute errors.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat this file as part of the library’s public API surface; verify usage sites and keep names/exports consistent.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying the lint-suppression section (the trailing bare symbol lines) without maintaining its intent
pitfalls.consequence: May reintroduce lint/type-checker failures in CI, or cause tooling to flag unused imports (depending on project configuration).
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the pattern’s purpose when editing; confirm CI/lint expectations.

pitfalls.mistake: Introducing circular imports by adding imports from modules that depend on `octodns.record`
pitfalls.consequence: Import-time cycles can cause partially initialized modules and hard-to-debug runtime errors.
pitfalls.prevention: Only re-export symbols from leaf submodules that already work independently; keep dependency direction one-way.

reading_guide.start_here: module-level imports and the trailing symbol-suppression block
key_sections: import block: one import per record/value/change/exception type from submodules
symbol-suppression block: bare name expressions to mark re-exports as intentionally used
skip_unless_needed: There are no functions/classes defined here; everything is import/re-export wiring.

related_files.relationship: Defines `ARecord` and `Ipv4Address`/`Ipv4Value` that are re-exported by this package init.
related_files.reason_to_check: If record export lists change, ensure the originating module symbols still exist and behave correctly.

related_files.relationship: Defines `Change`, `Create`, `Update`, `Delete` used in planning/sync and re-exported here.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changing change type names/exports here affects plan output parsing/execution logic across the system.

docs/conf.py.short_description: Sphinx configuration with logic to rewrite local links to the correct Git ref.
docs/conf.py.description:
This file is the Sphinx configuration entry point for octoDNS documentation builds. It defines Sphinx project metadata (notably `release` sourced from `octodns.__init__.__version__`), enables a fixed set of Sphinx and third-party extensions (autodoc/autosummary, coverage, napoleon, intersphinx, todo, myst, mermaid, RTD theme, etc.), and sets common autodoc defaults via `autodoc_default_options` and type-hint rendering behavior.

Beyond static configuration, it includes custom build-time Python logic to ensure that “repo-local” references in generated docs resolve to the correct GitHub URL for the version being built. It computes a `_source_base` URL that appends `/tree/<ref>`, where `<ref>` is determined by `_detect_docs_ref()`: it prefers the Read the Docs environment variable `READTHEDOCS_GIT_IDENTIFIER` and otherwise calls `_detect_git_ref()`.

`_detect_git_ref()` actively probes the local git checkout by executing git commands to determine the ref in a specific precedence order: branch name (`git symbolic-ref ... HEAD`), then exact tag (`git describe --tags --exact-match HEAD`), and finally short commit SHA (`git rev-parse --short HEAD`). If git is missing/unexecutable or commands fail, it raises `RuntimeError` with contextual messaging rather than silently producing incorrect/empty refs. `_rewrite_repo_local_links()` is registered in `setup(app)` as a `doctree-resolved` callback; it walks the Docutils doctree, finds `nodes.reference` elements whose `refuri` begins with any value in `_repo_local_link_prefixes`, and rewrites those `refuri` values to `app.config.octodns_source_base_url + refuri` using the computed ref-specific base.

key_constructs.name: _detect_git_ref
key_constructs.purpose: Resolve the documentation source ref (branch/tag/short SHA) by running git commands.
key_constructs.reasoning: The returned ref is embedded into the GitHub URL used for link rewriting; incorrect refs produce version-mismatched links.

key_constructs.name: _detect_docs_ref
key_constructs.purpose: Choose which ref to use for docs link rewriting, preferring Read the Docs' provided identifier.
key_constructs.reasoning: RTD builds depend on `READTHEDOCS_GIT_IDENTIFIER`; using it avoids mismatch between the built docs and the computed local git state.

key_constructs.name: _rewrite_repo_local_links
key_constructs.purpose: Rewrite doctree `nodes.reference` URIs that point to repo-local paths into fully-qualified GitHub URLs.
key_constructs.reasoning: This function bridges doc source link conventions and the hosted GitHub URLs so links work consistently across build environments.

key_constructs.name: setup
key_constructs.purpose: Register the computed base URL in Sphinx app config and connect the doctree rewrite callback.
key_constructs.reasoning: Sphinx calls these hooks during the build; the rewrite logic depends on having `octodns_source_base_url` available in `app.config`.

key_behaviors: sets Sphinx `project` and `release` using package version
configures Sphinx extension set and autodoc defaults
computes a GitHub base URL with `/tree/<ref>` for the docs build
uses `READTHEDOCS_GIT_IDENTIFIER` when present, otherwise derives ref via local git
rewrites specific repo-local `refuri` values inside the doctree during `doctree-resolved
raises `RuntimeError` when git execution/ref resolution fails

pitfalls.mistake: Returning empty strings or silently falling back when git commands fail inside `_detect_git_ref()`.
pitfalls.consequence: Hosted docs may point at incorrect or non-existent GitHub refs, breaking version-accuracy guarantees and potentially hiding build/environment issues.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the existing fail-fast `RuntimeError` behavior and precedence semantics for determining the ref.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing `_repo_local_link_prefixes` without matching the actual `refuri` values produced by doc sources or Docutils nodes.
pitfalls.consequence: Some intended links will not be rewritten (leading to broken links on hosted docs), or unintended links might be rewritten.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the prefix list aligned with how links are authored and how `nodes.reference` stores `refuri`.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the ref selection precedence between `READTHEDOCS_GIT_IDENTIFIER` and local git-derived refs.
pitfalls.consequence: Local builds and RTD builds may generate different link targets for the same docs content, creating inconsistencies.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the intended order: RTD identifier first, then branch, then exact tag, then short SHA.

pitfalls.mistake: Failing to normalize trailing slashes for `OCTODNS_SOURCE_BASE_URL` or the final `_source_base`.
pitfalls.consequence: Double slashes or malformed URLs can be generated, leading to incorrect link targets.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing `.rstrip('/')` normalization steps when modifying base URL handling.

key_sections: Sphinx configuration section: extensions, autodoc defaults, theme, MyST/intersphinx
_detect_git_ref: git probing precedence and fail-fast RuntimeError behavior
_detect_docs_ref: RTD env var precedence
Link rewriting: `_source_base`, `_repo_local_link_prefixes`, `_rewrite_repo_local_links`
setup(app): config value registration and doctree callback wiring
skip_unless_needed: Most of the extension/autodoc/theme configuration (only relevant if changing documentation build features rather than link/version logic).

related_files.relationship: Defines `__version__` used to set Sphinx `release` metadata.
related_files.reason_to_check: If docs show incorrect version numbers, verify how `__version__` is exposed/updated.

related_files.relationship: Documentation content and source links whose `refuri` may be rewritten by `_rewrite_repo_local_links`.
related_files.reason_to_check: If link rewriting appears incomplete or overly broad, check how links in the docs sources map to `refuri` values and whether they match `_repo_local_link_prefixes`.

insights.title: Fail docs build if git ref cannot be resolved; handle git execution errors explicitly
insights.problem: Docs source links could silently fall back to `main`, misaligning with the actual git checkout (or hiding git probing failures).
insights.root_cause: Previous logic swallowed errors and returned empty refs / defaulted to `main` without surfacing why ref resolution failed.
insights.solution: Hardened `_detect_git_ref()` to raise `RuntimeError` when git is missing (`FileNotFoundError`, `OSError`) or when commands fail (e.g., detached/no tag) instead of silently returning empty and falling back to `main`. Also normalized OCTODNS source base URL by stripping trailing slashes and improved git stderr handling to capture output for errors.
insights.lesson_learned: For documentation link correctness, prefer failing fast over silently generating incorrect links; normalize base URLs to avoid double slashes.

insights.title: Document docs source ref selection order and local build configuration
insights.problem: Contributors lacked clear instructions for building docs with correct GitHub source links, especially outside RTD.
insights.root_cause: Docs conf behavior around ref detection wasn’t well documented in contributor guidelines.
insights.solution: Added a Documentation and Read the Docs section and explicitly listed ref resolution order and guidance on setting `OCTODNS_SOURCE_BASE_URL` for forks/local builds.
insights.lesson_learned: When build output correctness depends on environment variables and CI context, document the exact variable names and precedence.

insights.title: Fix slash_commands workflow parsing and push target branch
insights.problem: The workflow failed to correctly parse command type arguments and could push to the wrong/ref non-existent branch for changelog creation, breaking automation.
insights.root_cause: sed regex only handled a single flag form and the branch-discovery/push logic relied on SHA->origin branch mapping, which can be unreliable for PR contexts.
insights.solution: Updated parsing sed regex to accept both -t and --type formats; changed push destination to HEAD:refs/pull/<issue-number>/head to ensure the push goes to the PR head ref directly.
insights.lesson_learned: CI/workflow branch targeting should be PR-context aware; avoid heuristics like 'find origin branch containing SHA' when the build runs for PRs. Also broaden input parsing for equivalent flag formats.

insights.title: Update templating tests to validate trailing dot removal and alias-zone pipeline behavior
insights.problem: Template output expectations changed due to new/modified templating behavior (process timing and trailing dot normalization). Existing tests did not cover or did not assert the correct parameters/outputs.
insights.root_cause: Tests were coupled to prior processor hook timing (`process_source_zone`) and prior templating variable formats (with/without trailing dots).
insights.solution: Adjusted tests to call `process_source_and_target_zones` for alias-compatible behavior, updated expected template outputs/parameter dictionaries, and added coverage for `TemplatingError` on bad placeholders and for `trailing_dots=False`.
insights.lesson_learned: When processor lifecycle hooks change, tests must be updated to use the new entry points; when adding formatting toggles, assert complete parameter values to prevent regressions.

insights.title: Add unit tests for root_ns_warnings and strict_supports interaction
insights.problem: New provider parameter `root_ns_warnings` could regress behavior (logging and exception-raising) if not covered.
insights.root_cause: Without tests, changes to warning/exception logic in `BaseProvider._process_desired_zone` might not maintain compatibility across combinations of `SUPPORTS_ROOT_NS`, presence/absence of desired root NS records, `strict_supports`, and `root_ns_warnings`.
insights.solution: Added `test_root_ns_warnings` validating: warning logged when supported but missing record, no warning when disabled; warning logged when unsupported but record exists, no warning when disabled; `strict_supports=True` raises `SupportsException`.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding a boolean that gates logging or behavior, test all combinations of related flags and record presence states.

insights.title: Add YAML output assertions for escaped_semicolons=false
insights.problem: Behavior could silently remain escaped (`\;`) when the flag was set to false, especially when the output is written to disk.
insights.root_cause: Prior tests only validated validation/errors for the escaped semicolons behavior; they did not ensure actual YAML file content matched the flag’s semantics.
insights.solution: Added a test that runs a plan/apply through `YamlProvider(..., escaped_semicolons=False)` and reads the generated YAML file, asserting unescaped semicolons appear in the output for TXT/SPF-like records and that list formatting includes `- ;` patterns.
insights.lesson_learned: When behavior affects serialization/output, integration-style tests that read the written artifact are more robust than only inspecting in-memory objects.

insights.title: Add regression tests for semicolon escaping behavior in YamlProvider
insights.problem: Semicolon escaping had edge cases for escaped vs unescaped semicolons and could regress due to parsing changes.
insights.root_cause: Provider logic mutates TXT/SPF record dictionaries during `populate`; incorrect key usage or logic gating can break output formatting.
insights.solution: Added `test_unescaped_semicolons()` which loads two fixtures (`tests/config-semis/unescaped.semis.yaml` and `tests/config-semis/escaped.semis.yaml`) under `escaped_semicolons=False` and asserts escaped `\;` output in `TXT` record `.values`.
insights.lesson_learned: When provider behavior depends on configuration flags and string-escaping rules, add fixture-based tests that cover both escaped and unescaped inputs—including edge values like a lone `;`.

insights.title: Harden changelog parsing by switching to single-doc YAML load
insights.problem: The changelog script previously used `safe_load_all` and then relied on positional parsing that could be brittle with the `---` front-matter layout.
insights.root_cause: Changelog entry files are structured as a YAML document wrapped between `---` separators plus freeform markdown text; using `safe_load_all` is less explicit about the expected pieces and can behave unexpectedly if the split/formatting varies.
insights.solution: Read the file as text, split on the `---\n` delimiters, then `safe_load(pieces[1])` for metadata and assign `md = pieces[2]` (with newline trimming). Also pass parsed `data` into `_ChangeMeta.get()` for more reliable PR extraction.
insights.lesson_learned: When file format is a known multi-part structure (front-matter + body), prefer explicit string splitting into expected segments over relying on generic multi-document YAML loaders.

insights.title: Fix ordering of changelog entries to avoid major/minor/patch inversion
insights.problem: Changelog entries could be sorted in the wrong order (e.g., major vs minor vs patch).
insights.root_cause: The sort ordering weight mapping was inverted relative to the desired semantics, and the script also needed consistent reverse sorting behavior.
insights.solution: Adjusted the `ordering` mapping and added `reverse=True` in the sort so that the highest-impact types come first while still sorting by time.
insights.lesson_learned: When sorting by multiple keys with custom weights, verify both the weight direction and whether `reverse=True` is required—small inversions can silently produce the wrong changelog ordering.

insights.title: Support explicit PR number in changelog front-matter
insights.problem: The script could fail to associate a changelog entry with the correct PR when a PR number was provided directly in the changelog YAML.
insights.root_cause: Earlier PR lookup relied on a git-based cached search; if that search failed but `data['pr']` existed, the fallback logic was inconsistent.
insights.solution: Allow `_ChangeMeta.get(filepath, data)` to return `data['pr']` when present, using the epoch datetime as a neutral sort time when PR lookup isn't needed.
insights.lesson_learned: If metadata already contains the answer (e.g., `pr:`), prefer it over derived lookup logic; always ensure fallbacks consult the provided metadata.

insights.title: Improve changelog script CLI ergonomics (manual PR, dry-run style, auto-adding)
insights.problem: Maintainers needed more control over changelog creation and validation, and dry-run behavior was not consistent.
insights.root_cause: The script’s CLI lacked flags for common maintainer workflows (manual PR override, auto `git add`, make-changes vs print-only).
insights.solution: Add `--pr`/`-p` to `create`, add `--add`/`-a` to stage the created file, and enhance `bump` with a `--make-changes`/`--dry-run`-like default behavior (print-only unless explicitly making changes). Also strengthened changelog `check` logic to parse changed files more robustly.
insights.lesson_learned: For internal tooling, align defaults with safe behavior (e.g., print-only) and provide explicit flags for side effects like `git add` and file writes.

insights.title: Add regression tests for allow/reject value filters with None value records
insights.problem: Previously untested behavior for ValueAllowlistFilter and ValueRejectlistFilter when a record has value=None (empty value) could lead to runtime crashes or incorrect filtering results.
insights.root_cause: Test coverage did not include a lenient record whose 'value' field is explicitly None.
insights.solution: Augment tests for both ValueAllowListFilter and ValueRejectlistFilter by adding a lenient Record.new(..., value=None) and updating expected record counts/names after filtering.
insights.lesson_learned: For filter processors, include boundary-condition records (missing/None values) in tests to ensure both correctness and stability.

insights.title: Test include_target opt-out behavior for filter processors
insights.problem: include_target behavior could be implemented inconsistently across processors, leading to accidental deletion visibility in target zones.
insights.root_cause: Behavior depends on both source and target processing paths.
insights.solution: Added `test_include_target` verifying records are omitted as source when include_target=False, but still present as target records.
insights.lesson_learned: For config flags affecting source vs target behavior, tests should explicitly assert both paths.

insights.title: Update Plan logger/tests for Plan.data meta support
insights.problem: Existing tests assumed Plan.data always had exactly one key ('changes'); logger/HTML assertions assumed summary strings had no meta indicator.
insights.root_cause: Test expectations were not updated for the new Plan.meta field and the modified summary output format.
insights.solution: Adjust assertions to expect 'meta' in Plan.data keys, and update logger/HTML output checks to include Meta=False in the Summary text.
insights.lesson_learned: When core output contracts change (Plan.data keys and rendered summaries), update tests to validate both backward-incompatible surface changes (new keys/summary substrings) and that default meta behavior is consistently represented.

insights.title: Add basic PlanJson smoke test for output schema presence
insights.problem: PlanJson behavior could regress without coverage.
insights.root cause: New PlanJson output was added; needed minimal schema validation.
insights.solution: Added `TestPlanJson.test_basics` that runs `PlanJson` into a buffer, parses JSON, and asserts expected top-level keys exist (`test`, `unit.tests.`, `changes`) and that the resulting structure has 4 top-level entries.
insights.lesson_learned: For new output formats, use lightweight structure assertions (parse JSON and validate key existence) to keep tests robust.

insights.title: Added safety-plan threshold tests for zone overrides (updates and deletes)
insights.problem: Zone-level thresholds must override provider defaults; existing safety tests likely only covered provider threshold behavior.
insights.root_cause: None (test addition), but implied gap: provider thresholds were tested without verifying zone precedence semantics.
insights.solution: Added `test_too_many_zone_updates` and `test_too_many_zone_deletes`, constructing a `Zone('cautious.tests.', [], update_threshold, delete_threshold)` and validating that `Plan.raise_if_unsafe()` uses zone thresholds when present.
insights.lesson_learned: When precedence rules change behavior, tests must assert both the threshold computation and the precedence switch (zone threshold lower/higher than provider/default).

insights.title: Validate Plan.data and underlying Change.data shape
insights.problem: Checksum feature depended on new serialization shape; without tests the format could drift and break checksum compatibility or produce non-hashable/unexpected structures.
insights.root_cause: Plan.data and Change.data were new APIs introduced for checksum; they required shape assertions.
insights.solution: Add TestPlanSafety.test_data verifying Plan.data keys/types and Change.data keys/typed values for delete/create/update.
insights.lesson_learned: Whenever you create a serialization format used for hashing/integrity checks, add tests that assert exact schema shape, not just presence.

insights.title: Add regression coverage for MetaProcessor.include_extra behavior
insights.problem: Without tests, changes to meta value generation could silently omit or misplace added extras.
insights.root_cause: New functionality (`include_extra`) was introduced without prior coverage in existing tests.
insights.solution: Added `test_extra` asserting `include_extra` is preserved and returned as the full set of values when other flags are disabled, plus asserting defaults for other include_* flags.
insights.lesson_learned: For config-driven processors, add tests that verify both default behavior and the exact output ordering/contents for the new option.

insights.title: MetaProcessor version output now expected from __version__
insights.problem: MetaProcessor include_version logic output format depends on version constant; tests were expecting legacy name.
insights.root_cause: Pep-8 version constant migration.
insights.solution: Adjusted expected values from `octodns.__VERSION__` to `octodns.__version__`.
insights.lesson_learned: When changing version sourcing, ensure plan output/meta processors and their tests align with the new constant.

insights.title: Update SVCB tests for list normalization and new invalid-type error messages
insights.problem: Tests and expected error messages assumed scalar string representation for list-valued SvcParams; after normalization, behavior changed.
insights.root cause: Schema change: list-valued fields now must be represented as lists, and validation error messages use the key name (e.g., ipv4hint) rather than the previous ip4hint/ip6hint style.
insights.solution: Adjusted rdata examples and expected `svcparams` structures to use lists (e.g., `alpn: ['h2','h3']`). Added a new test case where list-valued SvcParams are provided as strings and asserted that all corresponding `is not a list` reasons are produced. Updated IPv4/IPv6 hint validation expected error strings to match renamed keys.
insights.lesson_learned: When normalizing internal representations, update tests to assert both type and validation messaging; add a test that covers wrong-type inputs to ensure error reporting is deterministic.

insights.title: Release script now reads __version__ instead of __VERSION__
insights.problem: If `octodns/__init__.py` only defined the legacy `__VERSION__`, the release script would fail or create incorrect tags.
insights.root_cause: The project shifted to Pep-8 compliant `__version__` and the script needed to follow.
insights.solution: Updated `script/release` to grep `^__version__` from `octodns/__init__.py`.
insights.lesson_learned: When migrating public constants (`__VERSION__` -> `__version__`), update internal tooling and ensure backward-compatible definitions exist during the transition.
